summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Adjustment pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – Where the wife seeks the greater of either an equal division of the property pool or lump sum payment of $25,000,000 – Where the husband seeks the wife receive 50 per cent of sale proceeds from a Suburb P property after deduction of adjustment payments – Where the husband made significant financial contributions and the wife made non-financial contributions – Contributions assessed 38/62 in the husband's favour – Where the Court was not satisfied family violence occurred as alleged by the wife – Where the husband has significantly greater financial resources and earning capacity than the wife – Where the wife is primary carer of a child under 18 years – Where the wife's health issues have some impact on her capacity to work – Adjustment of 10 per cent in the wife's favour pursuant to s 79(5) – Final division of 48/52 per cent in favour of the husband. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Substantial parcel of property owned jointly in Suburb P – Where parcel subject to subdivision approval into three lots – Where parties gave evidence after completion of the trial that one subdivided lot had been sold – Where question of how to treat uncrystallised capital gains tax liability upon sale of the remaining lots. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Discussion of definition of “property” in s 4(1) of the Act – Discussion of the definition of “property” as extending beyond property recognised according to ordinary legal and equitable principles – Consideration whether statutory requirement in s 79(3)(a) of the Act to identify property does not extend beyond property recognised according to ordinary legal and equitable principles. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where there was dispute whether the husband's interests in a group of trusts and companies were considered his property or financial resources – Where the husband has interests as a discretionary beneficiary of a trust in relation to the trust's interest in the corporate group – Where the husband contended his interests were a financial resource – Where the wife contended the husband owned 15 per cent of a corporate group’s assets or had a “suite of rights” that constituted property with a value equal to 15 per cent of the total assets of the group – Where the wife's case was based on a “one transaction” approach to reading the constitutive documents of the group – Where the wife failed to precisely identify the existing legal and equitable rights and interests of the husband's “suite of rights” as required by s 79(3)(a) – Discussion of concept of property “of” a party to a marriage within s 79 of the Act – Where the Court is not satisfied the husband has “de facto” ownership over the assets of the trust or group. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficiary for consideration and administration of the trust is characterised as an equitable chose in action – Where the Court declines to accept the valuation attributed by the single expert to the husband’s equitable chose in action – Where the Court is not satisfied there is evidence upon which to value the husband’s equitable chose in action – Where the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficiary is taken into account as a financial resource under s 79(5) of the Act. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the husband has an inchoate chose in action arising under a provision in a company constitution that gives him a right to payment on issuance of a Withdrawal Notice to exit involvement in the corporate group – Where the wife claims the husband's right to withdraw is one of the “suite of rights” valued at 15 per cent of the total assets of the group – Where the Court is satisfied the terms of the company constitution indicate the husband's right is not assignable and is “personal”, not property – Where there was no evidence the husband had an intention to issue a Withdrawal Notice and exit the group – Where the wife failed to explain why an inchoate chose in action could or should be valued as if it were a realised chose in action – An inchoate chose in action can have no value – An inchoate chose in action is not an existing legal or equitable right or interest in property and cannot be included in the property pool – Where the Court does not accept the values attributed by the single expert – Where even if the husband's interest were property, the Court is not satisfied a sufficiently certain present value exists to include it in the property pool. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the wife failed to establish the husband's shareholding in the trustee company establishes beneficial ownership of assets held on trust – Where the Court is not satisfied the husband's shares in the trustee company can have a value derived from the assets of the group – Where the wife alternatively claims the husband's shares have a value based on his right to payment on withdrawal from the group – Where the inchoate contractual right to payment is not a right attached to the husband's shares – Where the Court is not satisfied the husband's shares have a value derived from his inchoate chose in action. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the husband has interests as a discretionary beneficiary of a trust in relation to the trust's interests in a trust external to the corporate group – Where the husband contends his interest is a financial resource – Where the wife claims the husband's interest is property that should be included in the valuation of the group of which she claims the husband owns 15 per cent – Where the Court rejected the wife's argument that the husband owns 15 per cent of the assets of the group – Where the wife fails to establish the husband owns one third of the trust assets based on terms of a Deed of Amendment – Where the Court is satisfied the trust is separate to the group and that the husband’s interest is as a discretionary object of the trust – Where the Court is satisfied the husband's interest in the trust is a financial resource and taken into account under s 79(5) of the Act. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the husband sought a number of adjustment payments to be deducted from the wife's share of sale proceeds from a property in Suburb P – Where the expenses are costs the husband has largely paid out of capital since separation or future costs relating to the [Suburb P] property – Where the wife accepted some adjustments and disputed others – Orders made for some adjustments to be deducted from the wife’s share of sale proceeds.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

INTRODUCTION

These are property proceedings under Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the applicant wife, Ms Franc (“the wife”) and the respondent husband, Mr Franc (“the husband”).
There was no dispute that the parties commenced cohabitation between late 2000 and early 2001, married in 2003 and separated on a final basis on 12 or 13 August 2022. The parties are not divorced.
There are three children of the relationship:
(a) Mr AJ, born 2004, aged 21 years;

(b) Mr AP, born 2006, aged 19 years; and

(c) AK, born 2009, aged 16 years.

The parties’ financial arrangements have some complexity, as will be explained in detail below. However, by the end of the trial the composition of the balance sheet had been substantially agreed (Exhibit KK).
The net value of the asset pool is between $23,065,026 according to the husband and $40,054,859 according to the wife. The principal reason for this disparity lay in the dispute as to whether the husband holds existing property interests in a group of trusts and companies originally called Z Group, now the Z Corporate Group, as well as an alleged one third interest in another trust, the BQ Trust, if this trust did not fall within the Z Corporate Group. The wife claimed such interests should appear as the husband’s property on the balance sheet of assets and liabilities. The husband argued they should be taken into account as financial resources.
In summary, the wife sought the greater of either an equal division of the asset pool, or a lump sum payment of $25,000,000.
The husband proposed that the wife receive a settlement sum equivalent to 50 per cent of the balance of proceeds from the sale of the property at O Street, Suburb P (“[Suburb P]”) after deducting a number of adjustment payments.
There was no dispute that it would be just and equitable to make orders adjusting the parties’ property interests pursuant to s 79 of the Act. It was also common ground that Suburb P should be sold. Indeed, orders were made by the Court for such a sale in 2023, as will be explained later. The parties agreed the value of Suburb P to be $37,000,000 at trial. As will be explained, since the order for sale in 2023, Suburb P has been subject to subdivision approval for division into three lots, one of which sold after the completion of the trial, although this did not change the parties’ agreed value for the entire property at $37,000,000.

BACKGROUND

The wife was born in 1970 and is 55 years of age. She is employed as an instructor.
The husband was born in 1965 and is 60 years of age.
Z Group has been a commercial group comprised of a number of companies and trusts that, broadly speaking, conduct a property development business. It was founded by the husband’s stepfather in 1976. As will be explained, Z Group through its various entities constituted a substantial business enterprise in property development, which was structured broadly to benefit the interests of members of the extended Franc family. There was no dispute that Z Pty Ltd, as trustee of Z Trust, was at the date of trial the head or principal entity, under which other trusts and companies lay and operated.
The name “[Z Corporate Group]” came to be used after a restructure in 2007. This is explained below in more detail, but changes in 2007 were emphasised by the wife. Bearing this in mind, in these reasons, I will refer to Z Group and Z Corporate Group interchangeably, irrespective of differences, unless the context requires a distinction to be drawn. None of the parties’ submissions raised a substantial point about differences between Z Group and Z Corporate Group, except to the extent the wife relied upon the restructure in 2007 as part of her argument that the husband currently owns 15 per cent of Z Corporate Group assets.
The husband has worked for Z Group, now the Z Corporate Group, for most of his adult life and is currently joint Managing Director with his brother Mr AV of a number of the entities within the Group. He originally reported to his stepfather, who died in 2024, and now to his mother, and a group called the Franc Family Council, discussed below. He is paid a salary of $115,000 per annum from BR Pty Ltd.
Despite adding considerably to the length of this judgment, it is necessary to describe the composition and activities of the Z Corporate Group, and the husband’s role within it, in some detail, to demonstrate the complexity of its structure and the number of Franc family members involved in its activities. The Z Corporate Group has been, or is involved in several property developments, of which the following have some relevance:
(a) Town BS development;

(b) Suburb BT development;

(c) Suburb BU development;

(d) Suburb BV development; and

(e) BW Street development.

A development project at Suburb BX, conducted by the BQ Trust, is also of relevance.
The parties met in Sydney in 2000 and began living together later that year or in early 2001.
At the time the husband held, and continues to hold in some instances, the following positions in Z Group: (husband’s affidavit filed 27 March 2025, paragraph 18)
(a) Director and shareholder of:
(i) BY Pty Ltd since June 1986, along with other directors. The husband is the sole shareholder. The entity was registered in 1984 and is the trustee of BZ Trust.

(ii) CA Pty Ltd from March 1999 until September 2013 when the company was sold. Mr AV was also a director, and the husband and his brother were each 50 per cent shareholders.

(iii) AB Pty Ltd since August 1993. Mr AV is also a director. The husband and his brother transferred their shareholding to Z Pty Ltd as trustee for Z Trust in 2007 upon the restructuring of Z Group. The entity is the trustee of the AC Trust.

(iv) BR Pty Ltd since March 2000. Mr AV and the husband’s mother are also directors. The entity is the trustee of the BR Trust. The husband’s 20 per cent shareholding was transferred to Z Pty Ltd as trustee for Z Trust when Z Group was restructured.

(v) CB Pty Ltd since March 2000. Mr AV is the other director. The husband held a 50 per cent shareholding until February 2007 when it was transferred to Z Investment Trust upon the restructuring of Z Group; and

(vi) CD Pty Ltd. The husband became a shareholder in June 1997 and held 5 out of 100 ordinary shares.

(b) Director of:

(i) CE Pty Ltd from October 2000, the husband’s mother and Mr AV are also directors. CE Pty Ltd is trustee of the CF Trust and the CG Trust.

(ii) CH Pty Ltd from September 2000 until September 2007 when the company was deregistered; and

(iii) AD Pty Ltd from November 2000 until December 2015.

(c) Alternate director of:

(i) CJ Pty Ltd from November 2000 until 2003 when the husband was appointed director along with his stepfather and Mr AV; and

(ii) CK Pty Ltd from June 2000.

(d) Discretionary beneficiary of:

(i) CF Trust, established in 1991, which held units in the CK Unit Trust, established in 1999 and which managed the Suburb BU development;

(ii) AF Trust, established in 1992;

(iii) AC Trust, established in 1993, which owned land at Suburb U;

(iv) CG Trust, established in 1994;

(v) BZ Trust, established in 1996; and

(vi) BR Trust, established in 2000.

In or around 2000, the wife was a full-time university student studying a degree. She says she was also working as a fashion professional and public speaker. By 2001 she was working in journalism and did some event promotion.
In or around 2001, the husband states he offered the wife a job in Z Group working on an “as needed” basis in marketing tasks. From 2002, the wife claims she worked full time for the business for approximately two years. I accept this is correct.
In late 2002 or early 2003 (the difference is immaterial), the husband purchased an empty block of land at CL Street, Suburb BV (“[Suburb BV]”) at a discounted price of $500,000 plus stamp duty. The husband received a discount as the lot was part of the Suburb BV development and his siblings also purchased lots on the same date to build homes. The cost of building a home on the land was $848,326. The purchase price and build cost was funded by $200,557 in savings of the husband and a loan from NAB for the balance. The wife states the value of the mortgage with NAB was $1,250,000. The husband’s stepfather was a guarantor for the loan. The husband met the interest repayments on the mortgage, which totalled $173,758 while the property was owned, and other financing expenses of $6,602. The parties lived at Suburb BV for about 12 months until the property was sold in early 2005.
In May 2003, the husband became a 50 per cent shareholder in CM Pty Ltd which provided mortgage broking services for the Suburb BU development. He purchased the shares with his own funds. The company was deregistered in January 2009.
In May 2003, the husband became a director and 25 per cent shareholder in CK Pty Ltd which was the trustee for the CK Unit Trust. The trust operated a real estate agency. He states he used his own funds to purchase units in the trust through the BZ Trust. CK Pty Ltd and the units in the trust were sold in October 2013 for total of $997,308. The husband received distributions of $144,500 and CN Pty Ltd (later known as CB Pty Ltd) received the balance.
In September or October 2003, a property at CO Street, Suburb BU (“[Suburb BU]”) was purchased in the wife’s name for $445,000. The property was an investment unit and part of Z Group Suburb BU development. The husband claims he paid the 10 per cent deposit and obtained a mortgage with NAB for the balance. The wife disagrees and claims she paid most, if not all, of the deposit from money she had been refunded from an investment in a Melbourne property.
It is convenient to determine this factual dispute at this point. The wife’s evidence about an investment in the Melbourne property was vague. She claimed in an affidavit sworn on 28 April 2023 that she owned the Melbourne property before meeting the husband. However, in cross examination she agreed this was not true. Rather in her trial affidavit she said it was better described as an “investment” as the property was not put in her name. She said that she had provided money to a friend, Mr B, to purchase the property, but she could not recall how much, nor at the time of swearing the affidavit, the address of the property, despite having lived in it for a period of something less than a year. However, Mr B gave evidence in the wife’s case. He agreed she had provided some money to him, but the Melbourne property was purchased by a company he controlled. He gave evidence, which I accept, that he caused the Melbourne property to be sold in 1997, and placed the net proceeds into another investment. Then about five years later in 2002, after being told the wife was intending to marry, he gave her some money, although he could not recall the amount, which related in some nebulous way to money she had given him to buy the Melbourne property. In cross examination, the wife also could not recall the amount, or where it was deposited. However, she tendered a cheque butt dated September 2002 (Exhibit U), apparently recording payment to her from the husband of $20,000, and described by the husband as “repayment of loan”. The wife submitted I should infer this was more likely than not repayment of the money which the wife claimed to have paid towards Suburb BU.
It is plain the wife never owned the Melbourne property. The evidence does not establish whether she ever had an enforceable proprietary interest in it. It is unclear what money she received from Mr B in 2002 and on the available evidence I am unable to conclude that, whatever the amount was, it was utilised to buy Suburb BU. It is worth adding that, even if the wife did provide $20,000, according to her own evidence, the husband repaid her.
Suburb BU was on-sold in November 2003 for $650,000. The total capital gain was approximately $173,398. The wife claimed the husband did not inform her the property was going to be sold and she did not know how the proceeds were applied, though she suggested they may have been spent by the husband on the honeymoon or to repay a loan to his stepfather. The husband claims the parties discussed several times a quick on-sale of the property and that the wife knew this would happen. He tendered a copy of the transfer to the purchaser dated October 2003 which the wife signed as transferor. I am satisfied the wife knew about and co-operated with the sale of Suburb BU. I infer the net proceeds were used for the parties’ expenses.
In or around 2004, a new workplace policy of Z Group was introduced which stipulated that only one member of each family couple could be an employee of Z Group. The wife ceased to be an employee of Z Group and instead began undertaking work for them as an independent contractor through her company called CP Company. She worked on website design and similar tasks until about 2009.
In 2004, the parties’ first child, Mr AJ, was born.
In January or March 2005, Suburb BV was sold for $1,985,000, and the husband made a profit of $384,166.
In 2006, the parties’ second child, Mr AP, was born.
In or around July 2006, the husband purchased a property at CQ Street, Suburb CR (“[CQ Street]”) for $3,350,000. A mortgage with NAB was obtained for the full purchase price and the husband’s stepfather was a guarantor. Both parties claim they were involved in the renovations of this property. I accept both were involved, but I am unable on the evidence to determine the level of involvement of each party.
In February 2007, Z Group was restructured, into a group of trusts and companies called the Z Corporate Group, comprised of Z Trust and its wholly owned companies and trusts which manage the property developments. Many, if not most, of the corporate and trust entities in Z Group have been integrated into the Z Corporate Group. According to the evidence of the husband, and as mentioned already, Z Pty Ltd remains the entity at the head of the Group and became the shareholder of these companies and a beneficiary under the trusts. There are, as will be explained, some other relevant entities which fall outside the Z Corporate Group, in particular BQ Trust. The structure and composition of the Z Corporate Group was set out in the expert evidence of Mr Nathan Toscan. It is unnecessary to describe it in detail here, but I will refer to aspects of it as necessary in the course of these reasons.
The husband is one of three directors of Z Pty Ltd with other family members. The husband’s mother holds a 40 per cent shareholding in Z Pty Ltd, and the husband and his three siblings each hold a 15 per cent shareholding. The husband, his three siblings and his mother are discretionary beneficiaries of Z Trust.
In August 2008, the husband sold the CQ Street property for $4,700,000.
In December 2008, the husband purchased a property at CS Street, Suburb CR (“[CS Street]”) for $4,620,000 by way of mortgage with NAB. An additional $1,500,000 was spent on renovations which was funded by the sale proceeds from the CQ Street property. In May 2014, the CS Street mortgage was refinanced with Q Bank. The parties lived in the property from about 2009 until 2015.
In or around 2009, the parties agreed for the wife to stop paid work and take on the homemaker role full-time with the husband responsible for financially supporting the family.
In 2009, the parties’ third child, AK, was born.
In or around 2011 or 2012, and again in 2013, the wife claims she discussed with the husband her return to paid work so that she would not be as financially dependent on him. She claims he made it difficult for her to return to work and was not supportive. The husband denied these claims and states he would have been “delighted” if she wanted to work. The resolution of this dispute, to the extent it is important, can await consideration of the parties’ contributions below.
In September 2013, CA Pty Ltd was sold and the net proceeds of about $1,974,407 were paid to Z Trust. Distributions were made to the husband of $627,203.41 and to the wife of $360,000 in financial year 2014. The wife claims she was not aware of the sale and distribution. However, there was no dispute that between 2009 to 2022 she received annual distributions that were paid into an account managed by the husband and applied to family expenses.
Between 2012 to 2015, the wife states she completed a course to become a playgroup leader. She went on to complete an apprenticeship to be fully qualified in 2017. She states she occasionally worked as a playgroup leader earning between $80 to $180 per day.
In or around 2016, the parties moved to the home (“[V Property]”), a Franc family retreat property owned through AC Trust in Suburb U, City CT. Z Group has undertaken subdivision projects in the area. The family lived there for approximately 12 months before returning to live at CS Street in 2017.
In March 2017, the parties purchased Suburb P for $22,450,000 as tenants in common but not in equal shares. The wife has 99 per cent of the legal interest and the husband has 1 per cent. The purchase, including stamp duty, was funded by a loan from Q Bank of $15,715,000 in the husband’s name, a line of credit for $3,656,541.90 with Q Bank in the husband’s name secured over CS Street, and a loan from the husband’s stepfather of $4,500,000 that has since been repaid in full. There are two mortgages on the Suburb P property, one to Q Bank and one to AU Pty Ltd. The wife did not contribute any funds to the purchase price. The mortgage to AU Pty Ltd attaches to the wife’s interest to secure the difference between the husband’s 1 per cent interest and his financial contribution to the purchase.
The husband and his brother are co-directors of AU Pty Ltd and the husband is the sole shareholder. According to the agreed balance sheet there was no loan money presently secured by the mortgage to AU Pty Ltd as at the date of trial.
The parties lived in the Suburb P property from late 2017 to 2021.
In November 2017, the husband sold CS Street for $8,000,000. The husband states that after discharging the mortgage, he invested the balance of the proceeds of sale with Q Bank.
In October 2019, to refinance the existing mortgage on the Suburb P property, Q Bank made available a credit facility of up to $18,200,000. The loan is secured by the mortgage against the Suburb P property. This is a liability shared by the parties, the husband as borrower and the wife being one of several guarantors and a mortgagor. A condition of the loan is that there be at least $5,000,000 in secured assets at all times; along with security deeds and guarantees provided by the husband, the wife, Z Pty Ltd as trustee for Z Trust, and AX Pty Ltd as trustee for Z Investment Trust.
The Q Bank mortgage on the Suburb P property and the accounts of Z Investment Trust (and BE Pty Ltd as the corporate beneficiary) are linked and organised to service the mortgage repayments. It was common ground at trial that Z Investment Trust assets should be treated as property of the husband. The Z Investment Trust holds an investment portfolio with Q Bank. The portfolio investments include managed funds, public shares and cash at times from the proceeds of sale of investments and income received from investments. The funds drawn out of the portfolio by the husband to service Suburb P, and also for the parties’ living expenses and legal fees, have been set off against the loan account of monies owing to him by the trust for funds paid into the trust to acquire the investments with Q Bank. However, according to the husband this arrangement has become cash flow negative as interest rates have risen. At trial he said the balance of the portfolios stood at about $6,164,000. The husband claims the cash portion of the portfolio used to meet the shortfall in investment income is nearing exhaustion and thereafter portfolio investments will need to be sold to service the mortgage. I accept this evidence.
In 2019 or 2020, the wife undertook training to become a an instructor.
In or around November 2021, the parties moved out of the Suburb P property and into a rental property at Suburb BP. The parties planned to undertake a complete rebuild of the Suburb P home and demolition of the Suburb P property occurred around December 2021. At the date of trial, construction remained incomplete.
In 2022, the wife began working casually as an instructor on weekends.
On 4 October 2022, Z Investment Trust borrowed $1,718,712 from the husband’s stepfather’s entity, AD Pty Ltd, to meet a margin call by Q Bank. The wife states she was unaware of this loan. The husband seeks orders that this loan be repaid from the proceeds of the sale of Suburb P, this is discussed later.
In 2023, the parties purchased reclaimed land adjacent to the Suburb P property and entered a 20-year license agreement with Transport New South Wales in respect of a boat shed and boating facilities. To fund this undertaking the husband borrowed $110,000 from his stepfather. A further $420,000 was borrowed by the husband from his stepfather to meet the parties' legal fees and living expenses. These loans have not been repaid to his stepfather’s estate. The husband seeks these loans also be repaid from the proceeds of the sale of Suburb P, this is discussed later.
Between 2023 and 2025, the Suburb P property was unsuccessfully listed for sale. According to the husband, the property failed to sell as a single lot, so the parties obtained development approval for subdivision into three separate lots in October 2024. As at March 2025, the plan of subdivision had not been registered with Land Registry Services. The wife disagreed this was the cause for seeking subdivision. She claimed the husband did not disclose the approval of the subdivision application and that his behaviour in nominating a sales agent and not cleaning the site frustrated the sale of the property.
On 23 October 2024, the Q Bank loan secured against the Suburb P property reached maturity, however the husband was unable to repay it as the property remained unsold. The husband had made attempts to organise with the wife a joint refinancing of the mortgage with Westpac from about early 2024, however the parties did not reach agreement on this application for finance prior to maturity of the Q Bank loan.
On 24 October 2024, AY Bank (for Q Bank) sent the parties a letter confirming there had been an “Event of Default” in respect of the loan facility and that the bank had the right to exercise its powers as mortgagee for sale of the Suburb P property. The balance of the mortgage as at 3 November 2024 was $18,347,706.
On 4 November 2024, the husband received conditional approval from Westpac to refinance the mortgage to $18,200,000 upon the bank being provided with an executed Heads of Agreement from the parties regarding separation of assets.
On 29 November 2024, the husband received a letter dated 15 November 2024, confirming that AY Bank extended the loan term of the mortgage to 15 May 2025 with a 2 per cent increased interest rate. The monthly repayments were approximately $123,687 with additional monthly penalty interest of $30,333.
On 3 March 2025, an order was made by consent permitting the husband to apply to Westpac to refinance the Q Bank mortgage against the Suburb P property and for the wife to provide a personal guarantee.
On 10 April 2025, Westpac denied approval for refinancing of the Suburb P mortgage as serviceability of the home loan was not shown on evidenced income. The Westpac refinance did not proceed.

RELEVANT PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Some procedural history is relevant.
On 28 April 2023, the wife filed an Initiating Application in Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia seeking financial orders for an equal division of the parties’ assets.
On 9 June 2023, the matter was transferred to Division 1 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia.
On 11 July 2023, the husband filed a Response to the wife’s Initiating Application, broadly seeking that Suburb P be sold and, after payment of various expenses, that proceeds be divided equally between the parties.
On 12 July 2023, interim orders were made by consent that continued up to the date of trial and included the following provisions:
(a) that the Suburb P property be sold, with each party to receive from the proceeds $1,000,000 as partial property settlement;

(b) that pending the sale of the Suburb P property, the husband pay:

(i) all expenses in relation to the Suburb P property, including mortgage repayments;

(ii) all expenses in relation to the wife’s rental accommodation and motor vehicle;

(iii) the legal fees of the wife on a dollar-for-dollar costs basis; and

(iv) $2,500 weekly to the wife less any child support payments.

(c) that the husband pay for the wife’s private medical insurance;

(d) that the husband pay the wife the sum of $26,800 as litigation funding; and

(e) that the husband provided an undertaking that pending the conclusion of proceedings or earlier order, he pay all school fees, extracurricular expenses and private health insurance for the children.

On 30 April 2025, consent orders were made for each party to receive $100,000 as partial property settlement. Payment was from matured bonds held in the Q Bank portfolio of BE Pty Ltd and distributed to the husband through Z Investment Trust.
On 1 July 2025, the wife filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking to vacate the final hearing dates on the basis that there were outstanding expert valuation reports. On 7 July 2025, the husband filed his Response opposing the adjournment. On 9 July 2025, the first day of trial, the wife’s application for an adjournment was dismissed. The absence of expert evidence could be accommodated by the allocation of additional hearing dates, and this is how the trial evolved.
Between 10 July 2025 to 4 August 2025, I delivered five interlocutory judgments relating to evidence to be relied upon at trial and granting a stay, pending further order, of the “dollar for dollar” costs order against the husband.
The final hearing ran for 11 days with judgment initially reserved on 29 August 2025.
On 20 August 2025, the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking orders relating to the sale of Suburb P and, if not complied with, for the husband to be appointed trustee for the sale. The wife filed a Response on 27 August 2025. The parties agreed that further hearing of the application be adjourned until after final judgment with the parties at liberty to relist on 24 hours’ notice.
On request by the husband, the application was relisted on 6 November 2025 and consent orders were made in relation to contracts for the sale of Suburb P. The Court was informed that contracts had been exchanged for the sale of part of Suburb P, being Lot 1 in the proposed plan of subdivision. It was foreshadowed that the parties would provide an updated value for Suburb P based upon the sale price for the entire property which was anticipated to take place in the near future. For this reason, delivery of judgment was delayed.
As foreshadowed, the parties provided a joint memorandum containing updated information about the sale of Suburb P. It was agreed the property would not be sold as a whole but in three lots. Prior to judgment, a contract for the sale of Lot 1 had been exchanged with a sale price of $11,500,000 with settlement to take place 15 days after the registration of the plan of subdivision, which the husband estimated would take place in February 2026.
On 11 December 2025, the matter was listed for mention on the Court’s own motion. Leave was granted for the parties to reopen the evidence. The husband’s affidavit filed 10 December 2025 was received into evidence, as was Exhibit NN, subject to relevance. According to the uncontested evidence of the husband (husband’s affidavit filed 10 December 2025, paragraphs 24 and 25) the mortgage to AU Pty Ltd will be discharged upon settlement of the sale of Lot 1. The Court was also informed that Lot 3 went to auction in December 2025, but was not sold on that occasion. Lots 2 and 3 had not been sold at the date of this judgment.
Subsequently, as foreshadowed on 11 December 2025, the parties provided a joint memorandum on 12 December 2025 concerning calculated capital gains tax (“CGT”) liabilities arising from the sale of Lot 1 (Exhibit OO).
The husband also sought leave in his interim application filed 20 August 2025 to introduce expert valuation evidence of Mr Cameron Johnston about the value of Water Vessel 1. This was not objected to by the wife and leave was granted.

Z CORPORATE GROUP – CATEGORY 2

The Z Corporate Group is comprised of a number of companies and trusts which manage property developments. In his report at paragraph 66, Mr Toscan described the Z Corporate Group in the following terms:

(ii) 100% of [CY Pty Ltd] and [CZ Pty Ltd] which are holding companies that have accumulated significant retained earnings balances through the receipt of distributions from the various discretionary trusts in [Z Group]. (Toscan report, paragraph 72(j))
The funds managed by CX Company represent value to Z Trust through units held in CX Fund and the CX Investment Fund which are managed by CX Company (Toscan report, paragraph 82(b)).
As mentioned earlier, the dispute about the nature of the husband’s interests in the Z Corporate Group explained one of the major differences between the parties about the composition of the balance sheet. At the commencement of the trial, it appeared to be at least one permutation of the wife’s argument that the husband should be found to be the owner of 15 per cent of the assets held in the Z Corporate Group for the purposes of s 79. By the end of the trial, although this argument appeared to be maintained, in final submissions the wife placed emphasis on a different argument, to the effect that the husband has a “suite of rights” constituting property with a value equal to 15 per cent of the total assets held by the Z Corporate Group. There was also a specific complication arising from r 129 of the Franc Family constitution, which provides a mechanism for Franc family members to withdraw from the Z Corporate Group with a payout. The husband denied he owns 15 per cent of the assets of Z Corporate Group. He contended he holds no more than an expectancy as a discretionary beneficiary and has no control of the relevant companies and trusts. He accepted that he enjoys a financial resource through the Z Corporate Group for the purposes of s 79(5), but of uncertain value. I will discuss these matters in detail below.
A further dispute arose about the valuation of the alleged proprietary interest of the husband in the assets held in the Z Corporate Group. The single expert valued what he described as the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficial object at $11,047,000, based on a valuation of the Z Corporate Group at $73,643,000. The wife contends the value of the husband’s interest is $11,234,000. The husband contended, if he is found to hold a proprietary interest, its notional value is $9,288,659. I return to this later.
The key constitutive documents of Z Corporate Group are:
(a) Constitution of Franc Family dated 19 December 2006 (“[Franc] Family constitution”);

(b) Franc Family Council Constitution (“Family Council constitution”) and Deed of Amendment (“Deed of Amendment”); and

(c) Z Trust Deed dated 22 December 2006 (“[Z Trust] deed”).

In order to resolve the competing arguments on this important issue it is necessary to describe and consider the constitutive documents in detail. In particular, the wife argued the constitutive documents should be construed as “one transaction” and a “cognate whole”. The consideration of the constitutive documents should be prefaced by observing that they presented many difficulties of interpretation, only some of which were the subject of submissions. No arguments were put concerning the certainty of relevant provisions or their enforceability. On the view I take, as will be explained, such matters can be put to one side. The following discussion only addresses the issues which have been raised by the parties, so far as they are relevant and necessary to the determination of the parties’ matrimonial cause.

Franc Family constitution

The husband is one of three directors of Z Pty Ltd, the others being his mother and his brother Mr AV.
There was no dispute that the Franc Family constitution was a contract between the company and its members, directors and secretary and between the members themselves (Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) (“Corporations Act”), s 140(1)). The Replaceable Rules in the Corporations Act are expressly excluded (r 1).
A company constitution has the status of a statutory contract with some special aspects. The distinct features of the statutory contract preclude the implication of a term from extrinsic circumstances, rectification on the ground of mistake or a construction based on an understanding or arrangement which was not recorded in the constitution of a listed company (Egyptian Salt and Soda Co Ltd v Port Said Salt Association Ltd [1931] AC 677; Simon v HPM Industries Pty Ltd (1989) 15 ACLR 427 (“Simon v HPM Industries Pty Ltd”); Bratton Seymour Service Co Ltd v Oxborough [1992] 13 BCLC 693). As a corporate constitution, it is to be interpreted literally because it is an instrument on which third parties might rely (Simon v HPM Industries Pty Ltd per Hodgson J at 433–434; Stanham v The National Trust of Australia (New South Wales) (1989) 15 ACLR 87 at 90–91; Ananda Marga Pracaraka Samgha Ltd v Tomas (No 6) [2013] FCA 284; (2013) 300 ALR 492 at [153]).
According to its terms, amendment of the Franc Family constitution may only be achieved by special resolution (r 39.1). A special resolution must be “passed by at least 75% of the votes cast by members who are entitled to vote on the resolution” (Corporations Act, s 250MA). However, for Franc Family the special resolution will have no effect unless and until it has the “Consent of the Guardian” (r 39.1). “Consent of the Guardian” is defined to have the same meaning as in Z Trust deed, being the consent of four out of five Primary Family Members (r 2.1(6); Z Trust deed, cl 1.1(7)).
“Primary Family Members” are defined as the husband’s stepfather, mother, the husband and his three siblings (r 2.1(15) and r 100.1). I note here that this definition is adopted in the Family Council constitution and Z Trust deed (cl 1.1(20)), which are discussed in more detail below.
The Franc Family constitution sets out governance, business strategy and distribution policies at r 4.1:
The Primary Family Members are concerned to provide for an orderly and appropriate regulatory structure to manage the affairs of the [[Z Corporate Group]] in terms of developing the wealth of the [Franc] Family, determining the projects in which the [Franc] Family is to invest or otherwise be involved, the distribution of profits and capital, the terms upon which any Family Member who wishes to withdraw can do so, the terms of employment of Family Member who is employed by the [[Z Corporate Group]] and other matters relevant to the welfare of the [Franc] Family.

The Primary Family Members have accordingly determined to establish an umbrella trust to be known as [Z Trust], the trustee of which will be the Company. It is intended by this constitution, the deed constituting [Z Trust], and the Family Council constitution to implement a structure for the [[Z Corporate Group]] to evolve over time, so that the direct lineal descendants (subject to withdrawal of a Family Sub-group from this arrangement) of the founders of the family, [Mr BF] and [Ms BG] can benefit.

Subject to the requirements of Trust law and the terms of particular trusts which form part of the Trust Group, it is the overall intention that, subject to those limitations, the [[Z Corporate Group]] will be administered as if the Company was the head entity and all other [[Z Corporate Group]] entities were subsidiaries.

Each of the Primary Family Members will be initial shareholders in the Company and it is intended that distributions of capital and income will be made for the benefit of those Family Members or entities associated with them. Each Primary Family Member shall be entitled to request the Company to facilitate the distribution of the amount determined by the board which is available for distribution which corresponds to his or her proportion of the shareholding of the Company. For example, if the amount to be distributed is $1 million and the Primary Family Member has 15% of the shares in the Company, then the Primary Family Member may nominate entities which would be entitled to receive $150,000.

...
Rule 4.1 makes clear that any inconsistency between the statement of general principles and a specific provision is resolved in favour of the specific provision:
If there is any inconsistency between the meaning or application of the general principles stated in this rule 4.1 and any other specific provision of this constitution, the provisions of the more specific provisions will prevail.
The Franc Family constitution provides a definition of the Z Corporate Group, as comprising two sub-groups, the “Company Group” and the “Trust Group” (r 2.1(18)), which are also separately defined.
The Company Group is defined as the companies set out in Part B of the schedule to the constitution, and any other companies which are at any time added to the list by resolution of the directors and ratified by Z Pty Ltd in a general meeting (r 2.1(5)). Some of the companies listed in the schedule have been deregistered or are inactive. For the purposes of proceedings, by joint letter of instruction the parties instructed the single expert that the Company Group comprises the following companies listed in Part B of the schedule:

The Trust Group is defined as Z Trust and all trusts which are set out in Part A of the schedule to the constitution and any other trust of which a company in the Company Group is a trustee; or which is added to the list by resolution of the directors and ratified by Z Pty Ltd in general meeting (r 2.1(23)). Rule 4.2 provides for Z Pty Ltd to have a number of functions:
The Company will be responsible for the following functions of the Company including the Company Group and the Trust Group:

(1) overall governance;

(2) overall business strategy and policy formulation;

(3) making investment, business and financing decisions;

(4) overseeing business planning; and
(5) setting the remuneration and dividend/distribution policies for Family Members.
Rule 4.3 makes clear that “Recommendations on the subjects referred to in rule 4.2 should be made by the directors and approved by the shareholders in general meeting”.
For the purposes of proceedings, the husband conceded that the following entities, in which Z Pty Ltd holds shares and which had initially been described as category 3 entities, should be treated as forming part of the Z Corporate Group and be valued as category 2 entities. The husband and his brother are directors, but these companies are inactive:
(a) CY Pty Ltd;

(b) DA Pty Ltd; and

(c) CZ Pty Ltd and its shareholding in DB Pty Ltd.

As above, the intention therefore is that the Z Corporate Group is administered as if Z Pty Ltd is the head entity, and all other Z Corporate Group entities are subsidiaries.
Rule 119 is important. It provides that:
(a) distributions of income or capital to one or more beneficiaries in the Trust Group or one or more shareholders in the Company Group must be recommended by the directors and ratified by unanimous resolution of the shareholders (r 119.1);

(b) each Primary Family Member must request a distribution by reference to their shareholding in Z Pty Ltd (r 119.3);

(c) in respect of any requests for distributions from a discretionary trust within the Trust Group, subject to any requirements of trust law and corporate law, the company, Z Pty Ltd, shall use its reasonable endeavours to request the trustee to exercise its discretion to effect distributions in accordance with such request (r 119.2(3)).

The reference to “directors” in r 119 is a reference to the directors of Z Pty Ltd “assembled as a board”. The references to “Trustee” however must mean the trustee of a trust in the Trust Group, not, or not necessarily, Z Pty Ltd. A request for distribution must be for an amount “which corresponds to the number of shares which that Primary Family Member ... holds as a proportion of the total shareholding in [Z Pty Ltd]” (r 119.3). It is clear that before any distribution can be made to a Primary Family Member from any trust or company in the group, that member must make a written request, and the fulfilment of any such request is subject to the discretion of a given trustee and must be recommended by the directors of Z Pty Ltd and ratified by unanimous resolution of its shareholders.
The Franc Family constitution does not allow shares to be transferred to a person who is not a family member (r 125.2).
In circumstances other than death, unsoundness of mind, or insolvency, a primary family member can withdraw, in whole or in part, from the Z Corporate Group by following the regime set out in r 129 of the Franc Family constitution.
This rule needs to be considered in some detail because the wife relied upon it as a separate basis for contending the husband enjoyed either an existing property interest in 15 per cent of the Z Corporate Group assets or a property interest with an equivalent value.

Rule 129

It provides for withdrawal from the Z Corporate Group by a “WFSG”, which is defined to be a withdrawing Primary Family Member or Related Family Sub-Group descended from that Primary Family Member (r 2.1(24)). The husband is a Primary Family Member. Relevant parts of r 129 of the Franc Family constitution can be extracted as follows:
129. Exit of Primary Family Member or Related Family Sub-group

Each WFSG shall be entitled to withdraw in one or more tranches a share of the value of the [Z Corporate Group] calculated by reference to the valuation as determined pursuant to Rule 129.4 (Reference Value). Each tranche must be a multiple of 25% of the shares originally held in the Company by or on behalf of the WFSG. To avoid doubt, there is to be a maximum of four tranches.

129.2 Notice in writing on behalf of the WFSG must be given to the Chairman of the Family Council (if it is then in existence), the Chairman of the Board of the Company and the Chief Executive Officer of the Company of the desire of the WFSG to withdraw from the [Z Corporate Group] one or more tranches as referred to in rule 129.1 (Withdrawal Notice). The Notice must be given by the Primary Family Member or other Family Member who has become the shareholder replacing such Primary Family Member pursuant to rule 128 or rule 125.3.

....

The valuation shall be made by a valuer agreed between the directors of the Company and the Family Member giving the Withdrawal Notice. ... The valuer shall so far as possible value the [Z Corporate Group] as if:
(1) it was one whole consolidated corporate group;

(2) the head company was the Company and all other companies and trusts in the [[Z Corporate Group]] were wholly owned subsidiaries; and

129.12 On completion:

(2) the relevant number of shares appropriate to the tranche being withdrawn held by persons from that WFSG in the Company shall be redeemed for the sum of $1.00 each pursuant to rule 128.1(3) and any Family Member belonging to that WFSG holding shares in other companies or units trusts within the [Z Corporate Group] shall sign a transfer of those shares or units in favour of the nominee of the directors of the Company and deliver such transfers to the directors of the Company;

It is important to record that r 2.1(16) defines the “Reserve Fund” as follows:
Reserve Fund means a fund established by the Company from the profits of the [Z Corporate Group] to be held in reserve to:
(a) be used to assist Family Members with welfare, emergency and unforeseen cash needs. This fund is to be managed by the Family Council under the supervision of the directors;

(b) be used for the purpose of financing the withdrawal of a WFSG pursuant to rule 129 of this Constitution. This fund shall be managed by the directors;

It is helpful here to summarise the salient aspects of r 129 for present purposes:
(a) It falls within the Franc Family constitution, which is a corporate constitution, creating contractual rights between the corporation and its members.

(b) It confers upon a Primary Family Member, such as the husband, a right to issue a Withdrawal Notice, exercisable unilaterally and at will, to withdraw, in whole or in part, from Z Corporate Group which enlivens a right to receive payment of a Withdrawal Amount calculated according to a set formula. The calculation is made by reference to the number of the withdrawing member’s shares determined as a proportion of the overall shareholding of Z Pty Ltd. This right extends to a Related Family Sub-Group by reason of the definition of WFSG.

(c) A member has no accrued right to payment of the Withdrawal Amount until a Withdrawal Notice is issued.

(d) The value of the Withdrawal Amount derived by application of the formula is reached by a valuation of the assets of the Z Corporate Group treated as one consolidated corporate group to set a “Reference Value” (rr 129.4, 129.6 and 129.7).

(e) The amount payable, as calculated, is to be paid within 60 days of valuation (r 129.8) but may be paid in up to four tranches of 25 per cent each of the amount payable (r 129.1).

(f) Payment is stipulated to be made from the “Reserve Fund” (r 129.9(1)).

(g) If there are insufficient funds in the Reserve Fund to meet the entire payment within 60 days, the liability can be met by Z Pty Ltd over three years by instalments paid half yearly (r 129.9(2)).

(h) The Chairman of Z Pty Ltd and the Chairman of the Family Council retain a discretion, if they form the opinion that payment over the three year period would “place strains on the business of the [Z Corporate Group]”, to extend the period over which payment can be made by up to four years (r 129.11).

(i) This means the satisfaction of the liability to pay a Withdrawal Amount to a withdrawing family member may take place over a period of up to seven years, and can include interest, if the directors determine interest is “reasonable” (r 129.9(2)).

(j) Payment of the Withdrawal Amount is to be secured against mortgages over real estate of the Z Corporate Group with “normal” terms (r 129.9(3)).

(k) The Withdrawal Amount is not paid in exchange for the withdrawing member’s shares in Z Pty Ltd, even if the proportional shareholding is used as the basis to calculate the Withdrawal Amount; rather each share is redeemed for $1.00 (r 129.12(2)).

(l) The issue of a Withdrawal Notice cannot be characterised as a simple formality. Rule 129 creates a right to elect between mutually exclusive positions. It fundamentally changes the status of the withdrawing family member by removing that person as a participant in the ongoing commercial activities of the Z Corporate Group and its potential financial distributions.

Z Trust deed

The Z Trust deed defines the trustee to be Z Pty Ltd (cl 1.1(19) and cl 1.1(25)).
The Appointor of the trust is defined as the Chairman of the Family Council (cl 1.1(2)). The Family Council constitution sets out that the Chairman is elected by the Council from council members. The council members are the husband, his mother and his three siblings. There is little other reference to the Family Council in Z Trust deed.
Schedule 1 of Z Trust deed sets out that Primary Family Members, that is, the husband, his mother and his three siblings, are capital beneficiaries, default capital beneficiaries, income beneficiaries and default income beneficiaries of Z Trust.
Clauses 3.2 and 3.3 establish the trust as a discretionary trust for both income and capital. Until the vesting date, the trustee may pay, apply or set aside the distributable income for the benefit of one or more, or none, of the income beneficiaries, in the proportions that the trustee determines in its absolute discretion (cl 3.2(2) and cl 3.2(3)). And on or before the vesting date, the trustee may distribute the capital income to one or more of the capital beneficiaries in the proportions which the trustee determines in its discretion (cl 3.3(1)). If the trustee fails to determine the distributions on or before the vesting date, then the capital is held on trust for the default capital beneficiaries (cl 3.3(2)), and the distributable income is distributed in equal shares to the default income beneficiaries (cll 3.2(8), 3.2(9) and 3.2(10)). The trustee may also in its discretion at any time prior to the vesting date, pay or apply any sum or distribute any property to or for the benefit of any beneficiary for their own use and benefit as the trustee in its discretion thinks fit (cl 3.3(3)).
Clause 7 sets out that the powers of the trustee are those conferred upon a trustee by law; and various additional powers as set out in the clause. Of these, cl 7.2(25) provides that the trustee has the power to “[c]ompromise, compound, abandon, submit to arbitration or settle a debt, account, claim or any other matter for the Trust Fund or the trust created by this deed including but not limited to releasing the payment of any interest or principal remaining unpaid to the Trustee”.
Clause 8 sets out the rights of the trustee. Clause 8.1(2) provides that the trustee is entitled to be indemnified out of trust assets against liabilities incurred “in the execution or attempted execution or as a consequence of the failure to exercise a trust, authority, power or discretion”; or “because it is Trustee”.
Clause 10.1(2) provides that the trustee, being a corporate trustee, must retire if it goes into liquidation or has a receiver appointed. Clause 10.2(4) provides that the Appointor may with Consent of the Guardian remove the trustee by notice in writing to the trustee. The Appointor with Consent of the Guardian must appoint a new trustee (cl 10.3(1)). As already mentioned, Consent of the Guardian is defined as the consent of four out of five Primary Family Members (cl 1.1(7)). Accordingly, the Appointor cannot unilaterally appoint or remove the trustee.
The Z Trust deed was varied by a Deed dated 21 June 2017, but the amendments are not relevant to the issues to be determined.

Family Council constitution

The Franc Family Council constitution is an unsigned document and appears undated. I note the Deed of Amendment, which is signed, states the date of the Family Council constitution is 9 May 2019. There also appeared to be in the evidence two versions of the Family Council constitution, one of which was attached to the Franc Family constitution. Some terms were materially the same, although the earlier version contained a different share buyout arrangement, though this provision was not relied upon at trial. The version put forward by the husband was said to be dated 9 May 2019 (husband’s Financial Statement filed 27 March 2025, “[MRF9]”).
It was the husband’s evidence that the Z Corporate Group is controlled by the Franc Family Council. The mission of the Family Council is described as follows:
The fundamental purpose of the Council is to provide a forum in which Council members can communicate their values, needs and expectations in respect of the family and the business and develop a framework for safeguarding the long-term interests of the family.

The Council serves as a structure to develop members’ involvement in the business, gives members a role and voice in the business and develops guidelines for family decision making.

Whilst business governance is the responsibility of the Board of Directors, family governance is the responsibility of the Council.

Effective family governance generates a sense of direction, values to live and work by, and well-understood and accepted policies that tell members how they should behave.

The Board sets the direction and policies for the Business and the Council sets the direction and policy for the family. While it is important that the Council and Board do not intrude on each other’s territory, the Board, management, shareholders and the Council must co-operate, co-ordinate their activities, and communicate well with one another in order to have consistent and mutually supportive goals, plans and policies.

(Family Council constitution, “Mission”)
The role of the Assembly is described as:
The Family Assembly is the vehicle for convening all family members on an annual basis to share information about the family and the Business, discuss direction, values, policies and current issues, develop social relationships with one another and celebrate business and family successes.

(Family Council constitution, “Family Assembly”)
The members of the Assembly are “[Mr BF], [Ms BG] and each Family Sub-group, including spouses, who are at least 15 years old”.
Under the heading “Relationship between Family Council and board”, the Family Council constitution states: “The Council creates policy for the family and the Board of [Z Pty Ltd] creates policy for the Business”.
Although members of the Family Council are also directors of Z Pty Ltd, clearly, the statement of mission evinces an intention to establish the Family Council as a loose family organ separate from Z Pty Ltd and its management of the trusts and companies in the Z Corporate Group. The Family Council is not a corporation, or even incorporated association, as a separate legal entity.
The Family Council members are defined as “[Mr BF], [Ms BG] and one representative of each Family Sub-Group”. The initial members of the Council are the husband, his parents and his three siblings. Each Family Sub-Group, that is, a member of the husband's family and that of each of his siblings, is entitled to elect one member of the Council. Council members serve a term of three years and are eligible for re-election. As mentioned, the Family Council Chairman is elected by the Council from council members to serve a term of one year and is eligible for re-election.
The Family Council constitution defines the terms “[Z Corporate Group]”, “Trust Group”, “Company Group” and “Primary Family Member” in identical manner to the Franc Family constitution.
Clause 4 sets out the share buyout process, which appears to be simply a repeat of the rules in the Franc Family constitution, including that shares cannot be transferred to a non-family member in r 125 and the exit and valuation mechanism in r 129 (above at [125]). The wife did not submit the terms of the Family Council constitution were inconsistent with the Franc Family constitution in this regard, or that they somehow took precedence.
Clause 5 provides that the Council shall ensure that Z Pty Ltd establishes the Reserve Fund. The Family Council constitution defines Reserve Fund the same as the Franc Family constitution.
Clause 6 sets out a distribution policy. The Family Council will request the board of Z Pty Ltd to pay a minimum annual distribution to shareholders as agreed by the Board. Requests for distributions will only be made by unanimous resolution of the Family Council. As mentioned at [120], where such requests concern distribution from a discretionary trust, under r 119 of the Franc Family constitution, the effect of such requests is expressly limited at the discretion of the trustee.
The Family Council constitution may be amended by any council member providing a written proposal to the Chairman, and receiving approval by at least two thirds of the Council members at a council meeting (Family Council constitution, “Amendments to the Constitution”).
By resolution, the Family Council constitution was amended by a Deed of Amendment in relation to the distribution policy under cl 6. The Deed of Amendment is undated, though the signed resolution is dated 22 March 2022. The deed was signed by Z Pty Ltd, AZ Pty Ltd and each of the Council members; however the deed was carried into effect by a resolution of the Council members, as required by the Family Council constitution.
AZ Pty Ltd is the trustee of the BQ Trust. This trust was the subject of a separate set of contentions by the wife, arguing that either the BQ Trust has been incorporated into the Z Corporate Group or that one third of its assets should be held to be the property of the husband. These contentions require detailed consideration of the terms of the Deed of Amendment, which can be conveniently set out here. Determination of the wife’s arguments about the husband’s interest in the trust assets can be delayed until the discussion about the composition of the balance sheet.
The Deed of Amendment contains the following recitals:
Pursuant to the [Family] Council Constitution dated 9 May 2019 (“Constitution”):
(a) the Board of [Z Pty Ltd] and [AZ Pty Ltd] are part of [Z Group] and sets the direction and policies for [Z Trust] and [BQ Trust] respectively; and

(b) the Council Members are responsible for the family governance of the [Franc] family and sets the direction and policy for the [Franc] family.

The parties acknowledge and agree that while it is important for the Board of [Z Pty Ltd], the Board of [AZ Pty Ltd] and the Council members do not intrude on each other’s territory, the Board of [Z Pty Ltd], the Board of [AZ Pty Ltd] and the Council Members must co-operate and co-ordinate their activities in order to have consistent and mutually supportive goals, plans and policies.
The Council Members wish to safe-guard the long-term interests of the [Franc] family, in particular, in respect of the business and investment activities of the [BQ Trust] to ensure that certain Council Members who are directly involved continue to receive some form of steady income [BQ Trust] or where income is derived from [BQ Trust] is distributed to [Z Trust] that those Council members receive such income in accordance with the distribution policy outlined in the [Family Council] Constitution (Distribution Policy).
Pursuant to the Distribution Policy the Council Members can request the Board of [Z Pty Ltd] and the Board of [AZ Pty Ltd] to pay a minimum annual distribution as agreed by the Board and such requests shall only be made with the unanimous resolution of the Council Members.
The Council members wish to amend the Distribution Policy for the purposes of requesting the Board of [Z Pty Ltd] and the Board of [AZ Pty Ltd] exercises its discretion to distribute income annually either directly from [BQ Trust] or income derived from the [BQ Trust] distributed to [Z Trust] for the benefit of certain Council Members including capital gains.
The Deed of Amendment at cl 3.1 amends the distribution policy at cl 6 in the Family Council constitution to additionally incorporate the following:
Specifically, and to the extent permitted by law, the Council requests the Board for the [BQ Trust] and the Board for [Z Trust], distributes income and/or capital gains derived either directly from the [BQ Trust] or where income and/or capital gains derived is distributed from the [BQ Trust] to [Z Trust] in each financial year equally between the following members of the Council:

(A) [Mr BF] or such other person as [Mr BF] nominates;

(B) [Mr Franc] or such other person as [Mr Franc] nominates; and

(C) [Mr AV] or such other person as [Mr AV] nominates,

to the exclusion of any other beneficiaries of the [BQ Trust] and [Z Trust].

The Council acknowledges that the request expressed above are not legally binding and are not in any way intended to fetter or pre-empt the exercise of any discretion of the Board for the [BQ Trust] and [Z Trust] in carrying out their duties and roles and that any decision made by the Board for the [BQ Trust] and [Z Trust] shall continue to be made in the Board’s absolute discretion or as otherwise agreed by the Board.
The amendment, in broad terms, provides for the Family Council to request to the board of the BQ Trust and the board of Z Trust to distribute income or capital gains from the BQ Trust in a one third share to the husband. The amendment expressly states the provision is not legally binding and does not pre-empt or fetter the discretion of the trustee for the BQ Trust and Z Trust. For clarity, the only possible obligation created by the deed is for the Council to make and maintain the request to the trustee. A request can only be made with the unanimous resolution of the Council members and there is no obligation imposed upon the trustee to comply if such a request is made.
The Deed of Amendment does not attempt to amend the definition of Z Corporate Group in the Family Council constitution.
The Deed of Amendment does not attempt to amend the Franc Family constitution, nor is it able to, because, as mentioned, this requires a special resolution and Consent of the Guardian (Franc Family constitution, r 39.1). There is no amendment therefore to the definition of Z Corporate Group and the requirements of the r 129 valuation and exit mechanism as set out in the Franc Family constitution.

One Transaction

In her opening outline, the wife submitted that the Franc Family constitution and the Family Council constitution should be read together as comprising the constitution of Z Pty Ltd. In final submissions, she developed this by arguing that the three constitutive documents, the Franc Family constitution, the Family Council constitution, as amended by the Deed of Amendment, and Z Trust deed, because of the contemporaneity of their execution or formation, should be construed as one transaction and a “cognate whole”. This approach had importance for her arguments about the existing property of the husband, so it is necessary to address these arguments, and it is convenient to deal with some here.
The wife argued that because the formal choice of settlor of Z Trust, Mr BJ, appeared to have been made merely for convenience, the operative intention of Z Trust through which the provisions of its trust deed fall to be construed are those of Z Pty Ltd as the other party to Z Trust deed, relying upon Colquhoun v Dronpane [2011] NSWSC 1500 at [15]; Re George Hardi Family Trust [2021] NSWSC 1584 at [19]. Those decisions concerned the identification of intention for the purposes of rectification of a trust deed. Rectification is no part of either party’s case. But I accept the Franc Family constitution is one source from which relevant intention could be inferred, to the extent such intention plays any role in the resolution of the wife’s arguments about the ownership of property by the husband. I also accept the intentions of the settlor in this case are not relevant, and there was no separate evidence about them in any event.
The wife’s argument continued with the submission that the three constitutive documents should be construed together and by reference to one another, with the result that the equities accruing to a party must be those arising from the transaction as a whole, relying upon Smith v Chadwick (1882) 20 ChD 27 at 62 per Jessel MR; Golden Mile Property Investments Pty Ltd (In Liq) v Cudgegong Australia Pty Ltd (2016) 18 BPR 36,121 at [67]–[70]; Raphael Shin Enterprises Pty Ltd v Waterpoint Shepherds Bay Pty Ltd [2015] NSWCA 406 at [68].
I accept the principle that several deeds may form part of the one transaction, if certain factual conditions are present: if they are contemporaneously executed, they can have the same effect as if they were one deed; if each is executed on the faith of all the others being executed and is intended to speak only as part of the one transaction. Whether the subject matter of an agreement is subject to more than one binding contract remains a question of fact (North Eastern Properties Ltd v Coleman [2010] EWCA Civ 277; [2010] 1 WLR 2715 at 2728), and regard can be had to context (Mainteck Services Pty Ltd v Stein Heurtey SA [2014] NSWCA 184; (2014) 89 NSWLR 633 at [86]). In addition, “if one is seeking to make equities apply to the parties they must be equities arising out of the transaction as a whole” (Manks v Whiteley [1912] 1 Ch 735 (“Manks v Whiteley”) at 754). I will call this the “one transaction” approach.
According to her Case Outline filed 7 July 2025, at paragraphs 70 and 71, the wife argued that reading the distribution policy in the form set out in the Family Council constitution prior to the Amendment Deed, with r 4.1 of the Franc Family constitution, “creates a consistent scheme for proportionate distributions of income and capital to the shareholders of [Z Pty Ltd] reflective of their proportionate shareholding”. She contended the Franc Family constitution and “the annexed” Family Council constitution take effect as a contract personally “between each of the shareholders, who in turn are also the directors of [Z Pty Ltd] and the members of the Family Council”.
The wife pointed to the terms of Z Trust deed, discussed above, which in summary permit the Appointor of Z Trust, being the Chairman of the Family Council to remove or replace Z Pty Ltd as trustee of Z Trust with the “Consent of the Guardian” who is defined to mean four of the Primary Family Members as defined in the Franc Family constitution. These individuals would also be the majority of the board of Z Pty Ltd. The consequence, according to the wife, is that the Family Council could “readily change” Z Pty Ltd as trustee of Z Trust if it was not willing to follow the request, or effect the policies of, the Family Council.
The wife then argued at paragraph 76 of her Case Outline:
The Court should find that the board of [Z Pty Ltd], in its overarching governance of [Z Trust], will as a matter of reality give effect to all policies and requests conveyed by the Family Council in circumstances where the same individuals comprise each body and where the Family Council’s control is otherwise embedded by its power to replace the trustee of [Z Trust]...
The husband disputes that the Family Council constitution is a legally binding contract because there are no signatories and no identified parties to the document. He points out that there are no enforcement clauses and many provisions simply replicate the terms of the Franc Family constitution. He argues that the terms of the document should be interpreted as “repeating the content of obligations that have binding force derived from a different source or as an unenforceable aspirational statement of purpose in which the relevant family members have joined” (husband’s written submissions, paragraph 60). He adds that the fact a version of the Family Council constitution was annexed to the Franc Family constitution has no bearing upon the question of whether it is legally binding in a contractual sense.
The status of the Family Council constitution is perplexing. Taken on its own, the question of whether it is a binding contract between, for example, the Z Pty Ltd board members or shareholders and others is to be resolved in the first instance by objective evidence of their intentions. The evidence of intention was scarce. There is a further problem, in that, as mentioned, the wife tendered two versions of the Family Council constitution, one attached to the Franc Family constitution and what appeared to be a later version (see above at [136]). The later version was different to the earlier version and substantially replicated material parts of the Franc Family constitution. As mentioned, the husband relied upon the later version dated 9 May 2019. The wife made no clear submissions about which version should be understood as the operative version at the date of trial. I accept the later version dated 9 May 2019 was intended to supersede any earlier version, and is the operative document for present purposes.
The fact that a deed was employed to amend the Family Council constitution is some post contractual evidence that the constitution was intended to be a contractual document, even if both versions were not signed. Post-contractual conduct is admissible on the question of whether a contract was formed (Brambles Holdings Ltd v Bathurst City Council [2001] NSWCA 61; (2001) 53 NSWLR 153 (“Brambles”) at [25] and [27]).
But, adopting an objective construction, I am unable to conclude the Family Council constitution has contractual force. It speaks to intentions but, assuming it is not inconsistent with the Franc Family constitution, which no one argued, it creates no additional rights and imposes no additional obligations which could be enforced against identifiable obligors or obligees.
Beyond this problem, the husband’s evidence is that the Family Council sets the direction of policy, whereas the Z Pty Ltd board is responsible for the overall corporate governance, direction and policies for the business of the Z Corporate Group. This separation is evident from the terms of the Family Council constitution. Whatever type of entity the Family Council may be, it is separate to Z Pty Ltd as a trustee company, as comprised of its members, board and constitution. It is clear that the Family Council constitution has never been the constitution of a corporate entity with the attendant special features as a contract between shareholders and company, and a board of directors. I am not persuaded that the Family Council can be characterised as a separate legal entity at all. It is an association of individuals, and corporate entities such as AZ Pty Ltd (which executed the Amendment Deed), which overlaps with, but stands outside of, the board of Z Pty Ltd. The Z Pty Ltd board and the Family Council have different roles. Only the board of Z Pty Ltd has the legal responsibility and well known statutory and equitable duties to make decisions which control the activities and assets of the Z Corporate Group as a structure of trusts and companies, even if those decisions may be influenced by the Family Council.
The division of roles supports the conclusion that the Family Council constitution was not intended to be part of the Franc Family constitution and was indeed more “aspirational” than contractual. The use of a deed to amend the Family Council constitution may have had the legal effect of incorporating the original terms into the amending deed. But this was not directly argued and in any event, I do not think such questions need to be resolved.
The acknowledgment in the Deed of Amendment by the Family Council that its requests are not legally binding and do not fetter any trustee discretion or the proper exercise of the roles and duties of the Z Pty Ltd board of directors also speaks against a document embodying contractual rights and obligations or treating the Family Council as in any way controlling, as opposed to influencing, the decisions of Z Pty Ltd or the distribution of Z Trust income or capital. The submissions of the wife to the contrary (above at [159]–[161]) are not persuasive. They plainly amount to arguing that the same individuals who constitute the board of Z Pty Ltd would decide to replace the trustee, in other words themselves as controlling the trustee as directors, if they failed to effect policies or requests they themselves had made through the Family Council. The artificiality of this contention is exposed by stating it. Moreover, the wife did not explain why, even if the Family Council was held to control Z Pty Ltd through the Appointor’s power to replace the trustee, this made any difference regarding an alteration of the spouse parties’ property interests under s 79, where she also accepted the relevant individuals were overlapping and identical as between the board of Z Pty Ltd and the Family Council, and neither spouse party controlled either deliberative body.
But ultimately, it seems to me that answering the question whether the Family Council constitution is a document with contractual force adds nothing to the resolution of the wife’s one transaction argument. Even if it did have contractual force, the husband pointed out that despite overlapping parties between the three constitutive documents, they are not identical. He argued this makes the “one transaction” approach untenable, according to Manks v Whiteley. The wife cited the decision of Finkelstein J in McVeigh v National Australia Bank Ltd [2000] FCA 187; (2000) 278 ALR 429 (“McVeigh”) at [31]ff where he relied upon American authority to the effect that identical parties were unnecessary “if the contents [of the instruments] were known to all” (at [33]). This decision has been widely followed (e.g. Insurance Australia Ltd v MOS Beverages Pty Ltd (2021) 286 FCR 1 at [100(4)] (Besanko and McKerracher JJ, Derrington J agreeing)) and I accept this statement of principle is correct. However, it does not assist the wife.
I am not satisfied that taking account of the context in which the three constitutive documents came into existence, the wife has established as a matter of fact that they form “one transaction” according to the principles articulated in Manks v Whiteley or McVeigh.
