It is not self-evident that the contents of the constitutions are “known to all”. Different elements of the wider Franc family are involved as between Z Pty Ltd and the Family Council. The role of the Assembly in the activities and deliberations of the Family Council illustrates the point. In my view, the constitutive documents, while obviously linked, to the extent they create legal rights or obligations remain separate. Construing the documents according to their ordinary meaning, it is clear in my view that the Family Council was intended to perform a separate and different role to Z Pty Ltd and Z Trust. A “one transaction” approach to the constitutive documents does not change this reality by permitting the Court to ignore structural and legal differences.
But ultimately it was not clear how the “one transaction” approach, even if adopted, advanced the wife’s case. For the reasons already discussed, I reject the contention that the constitutive documents, properly construed, establish “a consistent scheme for proportionate distributions of income and capital to the shareholders of [Z Pty Ltd] reflective of their proportionate shareholding” (wife’s Case Outline filed 7 July 2025, paragraph 70). There was no evidence that the Family Council in some way controlled the deliberations of the Z Pty Ltd board, even if the individuals comprising each body are the same or substantially the same. The Family Council clearly is designed to receive input from the Assembly. But in any event the Z Pty Ltd board must act as a corporate board subject to well-known equitable and statutory duties. The observation can be made here, as explained in detail later, that even taking the “one transaction” approach to construction of the three constitutive documents, does not in some way conflate orthodox principles for the identification of the spouse parties’ property and dissolve the fundamental differences between corporate and trust structures, or change the ordinary principles concerning ownership of property. The Court’s jurisdiction is over a matrimonial cause between the husband and the wife in relation to their property. As will be explained in detail below, the “one transaction” approach to the constitutive documents lent no support to the wife’s ultimate contention that the husband owned 15 per cent of the total assets of the Z Corporate Group.

OTHER FRANC ENTITIES – CATEGORY 3

For category 3, the husband conceded that for the purposes of proceedings all entities in this category, with one exception, were to be treated as falling under category 2, that is, to be treated as forming part of the Z Corporate Group.
The contentious entity within this category is the BQ Trust, a discretionary trust established by trust deed dated 4 August 2016. The trust carries out the Suburb BX development. The trustee is AZ Pty Ltd, of which the husband and his brother are directors. Z Pty Ltd holds 100 per cent of the shareholding in AZ Pty Ltd. There was no dispute that the husband is a discretionary object of the BQ Trust.
The husband denied that the BQ Trust forms part of the Z Corporate Group. He argued that Z Trust is only a beneficiary of this trust, and he himself has no control and no more than an expectancy as a discretionary beneficiary of Z Trust. Again, the husband claimed his interest was a financial resource, of unknown value contingent upon the trustee’s exercise of discretion. The wife contended the BQ Trust formed part of the Z Corporate Group, and therefore the husband held a notional 15 per cent interest in the asset that was to be considered his property for the purposes of s 79. I discuss this dispute below. An adjoining issue was whether the size of the husband’s interest in the trust was 15 per cent or one third.
The husband asserts this category of entity does not form part of the Z Corporate Group and is not subject to the Franc Family constitution or the Family Council constitution. He asserts the valuation and interest in this entity should be separated from any valuation of the husband’s interest in the Z Corporate Group.
In the event the Court determines the BQ Trust to be part of the Z Corporate Group, a second dispute arose about valuation. The single expert valued the BQ Trust at $5,791,000.
The BQ Trust deed replicates many, but not all, of the terms in Z Trust deed. The Appointor of the trust is the Chairman of the Family Council (cl 1.1(2)(a)), the trustee’s discretion to distribute income and capital to one or more or none of the beneficiaries, and provisions for distribution to default beneficiaries if the trustee fails to determine distributions on or before the vesting date (cl 3.2 and cl 3.3), are the same in both trust deeds. The terms of retirement of a corporate trustee (cl 10.1(2)), and that the Appointor may only with the Consent of the Guardian remove or replace a trustee are the same (cl 10.2(4) and cl 10.3(1)). The Consent of the Guardian is also defined the same in both deeds as requiring the consent of four out of five Primary Family Members (cl 1.1(7)(a)).
Primary Family Members are defined as the husband, his stepfather, mother, and his three siblings (cl 1.1(19)). This is consistent with the Franc Family constitution, Family Council constitution and Z Trust deed.
Schedule 1 of the BQ Trust deed sets out that the Primary Family Members are capital beneficiaries, default capital beneficiaries, income beneficiaries and default income beneficiaries. This is again consistent with Z Trust deed.
The BQ Trust is not listed in Part A of the schedule to the Franc Family constitution and there has been no disclosure of any Z Pty Ltd board resolution, or ratifying members’ resolution of the company that formally incorporates the BQ Trust into the Z Corporate Group in any way. AZ Pty Ltd is not part of the Company Group listed in Part B of the schedule and has not been added by resolution of directors and members of the company. On this basis, the BQ Trust does not fall within the Z Corporate Group.
However, the wife argues that this is not determinative. The BQ Trust was established in 2016, much later than the Franc Family constitution dated 2006. She contends that by the Deed of Amendment to the Family Council constitution, there is evidence that the BQ Trust has been incorporated into the Z Corporate Group as contemplated by the Franc Family constitution.
She contends that when the recitals to the Deed of Amendment, above at [150], are read in the context of the Family Council and Z Pty Ltd overseeing the administration of the Z Corporate Group, that is positive evidence of the BQ Trust at some unspecified point in time being incorporated into, and now forming part of, the Z Corporate Group.
She argues that supporting this contention is the consistency between the terms of the BQ Trust deed and Z Trust deed, which is subject to the overall control of the Family Council through the office and powers of the Appointor, being the Chairman of the Family Council.
She again argued that although the terms of the Deed of Amendment do not act as a fetter upon the exercise of discretion by the trustee, the practical reality was that distribution requests by the Family Council will be carried into effect, given the board of Z Pty Ltd is comprised of Council members who have the power to replace the trustee if they choose to do so.
In the event the BQ Trust is determined to fall under the scope of the Z Corporate Group entities, then the wife argues the value of the trust is to be taken into account for the purposes of valuing the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd.
The husband argued that in circumstances where Z Pty Ltd holds 100 per cent of the shareholding in AZ Pty Ltd, and where AZ Pty Ltd acts as a bare trustee, the shareholding of Z Pty Ltd has no material value, and importantly, is not a proxy for the value of the assets of the BQ Trust.
I will return to these arguments below.

PART VIII

Part VIII of the Act sets out the legislative provisions relating to property orders that may be sought when parties are or were married. The central provision is s 79 of the Act, which gives the Court power to make such orders for alteration of property interests as it considers appropriate.
These proceedings were heard after 10 June 2025, so the amendments to the Act brought into effect by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) apply.
Section 79(1) empowers the Court to make such orders as it considers appropriate in “altering the interests of the parties to the marriage” in “the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them”.
Recently in B Pty Ltd & Anker [2025] FedCFamC1A 85; (2025) 71 Fam LR 358 (“B Pty Ltd & Anker”) at [15] the Full Court reaffirmed the jurisdictional boundaries of s 79:
The breadth of s 79 is limited by the requirement that the proceedings are a “matrimonial cause” as defined in subpara (ca) in s 4 of the Act. This limit is imposed by s 39 of the Act (Russell v Russell (1976) 134 CLR 495 at 538; [1903] ArgusLawRp 46; 9 ALR 103 at 137 1 Fam LR 11,133 at 11,162 (Russell); In the Marriage of K J and J M Fisher [1986] HCA 61; (1986) 161 CLR 438 67 ALR 513 11 Fam LR 11 (Fisher); Ascot Investments Pty Ltd v Harper (1981) 148 CLR 337; 33 ALR 631; 6 Fam LR 591 (Ascot Investments)) although, in this case, it is by a proclamation under s 41 of the Act because this is an appeal from the Family Court of Western Australia. In Russell, Gibbs J said that the only way the relevant part of the definition of “matrimonial cause” can fit within constitutional bounds is if it is limited to “proceedings between the parties to a marriage” (at CLR 528; ALR 129; Fam LR 11,155), because “conferring a jurisdiction unlimited as to parties ... travels beyond the marriage power” (at CLR 541; ALR 139; Fam LR 11,164; see also R v Ross-Jones; Ex parte Beaumont [1979] HCA 5; (1979) 141 CLR 504 at 510; [1979] HCA 5; 23 ALR 179 at 183–4 [1979] HCA 5; 4 Fam LR 598 at 602). Importantly, the “section can only have a valid application with respect to a claim based on circumstances arising out of the marriage relationship” (Dougherty v Dougherty [1987] HCA 33; (1987) 163 CLR 278 at 286; [1987] HCA 33; 72 ALR 550 at 554 [1987] HCA 33; 11 Fam LR 577 at 580,, citing Russell at CLR 525, 528, 542–3, 552–[1897] ArgusLawRp 76; 3; ALR 126, 129, 143, 153; Fam LR 11,152, 11,155, 11,169, 11,179). That is, “the rights and duties which the ... Court may validly create or define under the section are confined to those which have their basis in the marital relationship” (Dougherty at CLR 288; ALR 556; Fam LR 582).
In my view, these jurisdictional boundaries must be kept clearly in mind in circumstances where a party claims that elements of the matrimonial pool of assets intersect with the rights and interests of strangers to the marriage, and property is held in or owned by legal entities like trust or corporate structures. The Court has a range of statutory powers which can be used to impinge upon the interests of third parties in particular circumstances, such as s 106B, and the provisions of Pt VIIIAA. None of these are relied upon in the wife’s case. Rather, her claims to incorporate a percentage of the Z Corporate Group assets or the BQ Trust, relied upon the statutory definition of “property” in s 4(1), the wording of s 79, and in particular the ambit of the collocation “the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them”.
“Property” is defined in s 4(1) of the Act as follows:
... property to which those [parties to a marriage] are, or [a] party is, as the case may be, entitled, whether in possession or reversion; ...
This definition has been described as “indicative and descriptive of every possible interest” a party can have (Duff and Duff (1977) FLC 90-217 (“Duff”) at 76,133; Tomaras & Tomaras [2021] FedCFamC1A 82; (2021) 64 Fam LR 237 (“Tomaras”) at [65], [174]). Although Watts J was in the minority in Tomaras, he uncontroversially explained at [185] that the definition of “property” in s 4(1) is exhaustive, and limited to only those assets which would be recognised at common law and in equity as “property”, and to property to which the parties are, or a party is, entitled whether in possession or reversion. In Duff the Full Court explained that the expression “in possession or reversion” was an adverbial phrase qualifying the verb “entitled”, that is, it describes the entitlement and not the property.
The majority in Tomaras (Ainslie-Wallace and Aldridge JJ) went on the explain at [66]:
Nonetheless it has been said that “[t]he question [of] whether a particular right or entitlement can or should be categorised as property has been one of continual difficulty which has troubled courts on many occasions”, and to answer that question it is “necessary in each case to first examine and carefully identify the precise nature of the particular entitlement in question” (Perrett and Perrett [1989] FamCA 56; (1989) 13 Fam LR 464 at 467; (1990) FLC 92-101 at 77,659 (Perrett)).
They then cited the decision in Mullane v Mullane [1983] HCA 4; (1983) 158 CLR 436 (“Mullane”) at 445 where the High Court distinguished an interest in property from a personal right, and stated alienability was not essential to a proprietary right:
An interest in property is a right of a proprietary nature, not a mere personal right: Stow v. Mineral Holdings (Aust.) Pty. Ltd.; Reg. v. Toohey; Ex parte Meneling Station Pty. Ltd. It does not exclude every interest which is not assignable or transferable (cf. per Mason J. in Meneling Station). Thus an order under s. 79 may give rise to an interest in property which is defeasible on assignment or transfer to a third party, or on the occurrence of some other event, or which the holder is enjoined from assigning or transferring.

(Footnotes omitted)
In Tomaras at [92] the majority also observed in relation to the concept of an “interest”:
The word “interest” like the word “property” is a word of many potential meanings and the word must take its meaning from the specific context in which it is used (Commissioner of Stamp Duties (Qld) v Livingston [1964] UKPC 2; [1965] AC 694; (1964) 112 CLR 12 at 712-713; [1964] UKPC 2; [1965] ALR 803; [1964] 3 All ER 692; Raymond Saul & Co (a firm) v Holden (as personal representative of Hemming (dec’d)) [2009] Ch 313; [2008] EWHC 2731 (Ch) at [55]).
In Stanford v Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108 (“Stanford”) the High Court made clear at [37], when considering the pre-amendment version of s 79, that it was necessary to begin consideration of whether it is just and equitable to make a property settlement order by identifying, according to ordinary common law and equitable principles, the existing legal and equitable interests of the parties in property, as well as their liabilities and financial resources at the date of the hearing (see Bevan & Bevan [2013] FamCAFC 116; (2013) FLC 93-545 (“Bevan”) at [72]–[73]).
Section 79(3) of the Act now provides that:
(3) In considering what order (if any) should be made under this section in property settlement proceedings, the court:
(a) is to identify:
(i) the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(ii) the existing liabilities of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and
(b) is to take into account (except for the purpose of making an order with respect to the ownership of property that is a companion animal):
(i) the considerations set out in subsection (4) (considerations relating to contributions); and
(ii) the considerations set out in subsection (5) (considerations relating to current and future circumstances).
Section 79(3)(a) imposes an express statutory requirement. The Full Court has explained that s 79(3) makes clear “[o]nly the existing property of the parties is to be identified and only that existing property is to be divided or adjusted” (Shinohara & Shinohara [2025] FedCFamC1A 126 (“Shinohara”) at [121]–[127]). A number of observations should be made. The qualifying phrase “according to ordinary common law and equitable principles” does not appear in s 79(3), the wording of which is limited to “existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property” (pointed out by Carew J in Caldwell & Caldwell [2025] FedCFamC1F 506 (“Caldwell”) at [87]). This could be read to include rights or interests which are found to fall within the definition of property in s 4(1) of the Act, but do not answer the description of property “according to ordinary common law and equitable principles”, a possibility which has been recognised in some authorities such as Kennon v Spry (2008) 238 CLR 366 (“Spry”) (see below at [222]–[223]). This seemed to be the position of the wife, who argued the husband enjoyed a “suite of rights” amounting to property, and there was no reason to limit the embrace of the definition of property in s 4(1) (below at [229]–[235]).
But it is difficult to conceive of rights or interests in property which meet the description “legal and equitable” in s 79(3)(a) but are not identified “according to ordinary common law and equitable principles”. Neither party submitted the amended version of s 79 changed this established criterion as the starting point to identify the property interests of parties to a marriage. As Ainslie-Wallace and Aldridge JJ commented in Tomaras (above at [195]) it has long been recognised that the question whether a particular right or entitlement should be categorised as property has been a source of continual difficulty. The decision in Spry made clear there needs to be some focus upon the statutory collocation of wording under consideration here, namely, “the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties” (see below at [222]). Construing this collocation in its context requires some attention to the use of the preposition “in”. The relevant right or interest must be “in” any property, with the consequence, in my view, the statutory collocation could only mean a proprietary right or interest. Since it is the obvious purpose of s 79(3)(a) to identify the property available for division, there is now reason to doubt it is open to construe the definition of “property” as extending beyond property recognised according to the ordinary legal and equitable principles of the general law. This conclusion accords with the decision of the Full Court in Shinohara (above at [200]).
Section 79(2) of the Act provides that:
The court must not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
Stanford commented at [36] on the meaning of “just and equitable” as follows:
The expression "just and equitable" is a qualitative description of a conclusion reached after examination of a range of potentially competing considerations. It does not admit of exhaustive definition. It is not possible to chart its metes and bounds...
(Footnotes omitted)
The requirement to make an order that is just and equitable permeates the entire decision-making process and is not a threshold issue (Martin & Newton [2011] FamCAFC 233; (2011) FLC 93-490 at [306]; Bevan at [62], [86]). Here the parties accept it would be just and equitable to make some form of adjustment. However, the Court must also be satisfied that the final proposed orders are just and equitable, and I will return to this below.
Subsections 79(4) and 79(5) prescribe a range of matters which the Court is required to consider. They are discussed below. Stanford at [40] also made clear that “[t]o conclude that making an order is ‘just and equitable’ only because of and by reference to various matters in [s 79(4) and s 79(5)], without a separate consideration of [s 79(2)] would be to conflate the statutory requirements and ignore the principles laid down by the Act”. This statement was made about the pre-amendment version of s 79, but nothing in the amended version of s 79 suggests it does not continue to apply.
Several other fundamental points should be made. Under Australian law the question of whether it is just and equitable to make an order “is not to be answered by assuming that the parties’ rights or interests in marital property are or should be different” from those at the time when the discretion may be exercised (Stanford at [37]–[39], [50]; Wirth v Wirth [1956] HCA 71; (1956) 98 CLR 228 at 231–232 and 247–248).
There is no presumption of equal sharing of property interests or community property in Australian law (Mallet v Mallet [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605). Unless and until property rights or interests are adjusted pursuant to s 79, one spouse has no inchoate or other proprietary interest in the exclusive property owned by the other spouse (Bevan at [80]; Lin & Ruan [2021] FamCAFC 90; (2021) FLC 94-024 at [41], [48]–[49]; Sarto & Sarto [2022] FedCFamC1A 16; (2022) 65 Fam LR 605 (“Sarto”) at [19]; Agnarsson & Agnarsson (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 407 at [46]; B Pty Ltd & Anker at [16]). It is wrong to assume that one spouse enjoys an inchoate interest in the exclusive property of the other spouse “simply because it was acquired during the marriage” (Sarto at [24]). Until the Court exercises its discretion to adjust property interests and unless restrained by an injunction made under s 114(1) of the Act, the spouses may each exert the full measure of their property rights against third parties and one another (Kartal & Templeman [2022] FedCFamC1A 46 at [53]).
The Court is required to make orders which are not only “just and equitable” but also “appropriate” (Zao & Lee [2019] FamCAFC 169 at [48]; Aitken & Aitken [2023] FedCFamC1A 69; (2023) FLC 94-142 at [59]). Section 80 grants specific powers to make a range of different orders to adjust property interests. Section 81 reflects a policy of making orders which finally determine the financial relationship between the parties and avoid further proceedings.

ASSETS, LIABILITIES AND FINANCIAL RESOURCES AT THE DATE OF THE HEARING

I turn then, to the identification of the parties’ property, liabilities, and financial resources at the date of the hearing, according to ordinary principles of law and equity.
A joint balance sheet was tendered by the parties, which became Exhibit KK. Many items were agreed. In relation to disputed items, I express my conclusions as follows, noting the reference to item numbers is a reference to the item number on Exhibit KK.

Items 1 and 52 – Suburb P

As at the date of trial, the value of Suburb P was agreed to be $37,000,000. As mentioned, Lot 1 of the overall property has been sold for $11,500,000, but Lots 2 and 3 are presently unsold. Despite the sale of Lot 1, there was no agreed basis to diverge from the agreed overall value of $37,000,000, which I will adopt for the purposes of this judgment. Neither party requested an adjournment of the proceedings pursuant to s 79AA(5) to permit the completion of the sale process for the remaining lots.
As also mentioned, the parties own Suburb P as tenants in common but not in equal shares. The purchase was funded by the husband, using the loan of $18,273,789 from Q Bank, which is being serviced by the husband alone, and a loan from his stepfather which has been repaid. Borrowed money normally becomes the beneficial property of the borrower and the lender is left with an in personam right, secured or unsecured, of repayment (Twinsectra Ltd v Yardley [2002] UKHL 12; [2002] 2 AC 164 at [68]). In that sense Suburb P was acquired using money of the husband. However, for the purpose of identifying the parties’ legal and equitable interests in property, there was no dispute that the wife owns 99 per cent of Suburb P legally and beneficially while the husband owns one per cent. While the husband borrowed the funds for purchase, the wife gave a mortgage over her interest as security and a personal guarantee. Both parties executed mortgage [...], dated June 2017, in favour of Q Bank.
Putting to one side situations where the spouse providing the purchase monies may have a proprietary interest in the property under a resulting or constructive trust, the property in question must be an existing asset of the spouse who is the owner, unless and until the Court exercises its power to divide the parties’ assets by adjusting the legal or equitable interests. Although the husband contributed the entire purchase price, I note in passing that here there appears to be no reason why a presumption of advancement in favour of the wife would not apply to the purchase of Suburb P, as between the spouse parties.
For these reasons, although there was no dispute Suburb P is a joint asset, for the purposes of settling the balance sheet, I consider the ownership of Suburb P and the loans secured by mortgage should be attributed to each of the parties according to the percentage proportions of their legal ownership and beneficial ownership. Although the husband alone entered into the loans to purchase Suburb P, the wife executed the mortgage in favour of Q Bank and thus remains jointly liable for the outstanding loan balance which it secures. This approach is consistent with the requirements of s 79(3). I note here that the wife’s guarantee, which is a contingent liability, was not included by the parties on the agreed balance sheet.
Despite the sale of Lot 1, the parties did not move from the agreed value of Suburb P at $37,000,000, nor did they provide an updated balance sheet reflecting the sale. If the sale of Lot 1 is reflected on the balance sheet, the agreed value for Suburb P of $37,000,000 will have to be reduced by $11,500,000, leaving a figure of $25,500,000 for item 1. The affidavit evidence provided by the husband on 11 December 2025, which became evidence in the final hearing, estimated approximately $10,161,526 would be received as net sale proceeds from the sale of Lot 1. The husband also gave evidence that the net proceeds will be applied to reduce the balance of the Q Bank mortgage to approximately $8,050,041 (item 52) following settlement of the sale of Lot 1.
The figure for net proceeds also includes a deduction for the selling agent’s commission of $189,750. This will have an impact on item 54 on the balance sheet, where the parties allowed a total for agent’s commission of $610,500. The figure for net proceeds also included a payment of $22,814 to BN Lawyers. This amount appeared separately on the balance sheet as item 55. I will return to items 54 and 55 below.

Item 25 – Z Corporate Group assets

Item 25 is one of the central areas of dispute between the parties, already touched upon above. In summary, the wife claims that the Court should find 15 per cent of the total assets of the Z Corporate Group are property of the husband, or other proprietary rights of the same value.
As already mentioned, the Court has a statutory obligation to identify existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property. The claim that a spouse party owns a percentage of numerous assets legally owned by a range of other entities, such as corporations, whether as trustees or in their own right, and in which third parties have interests, has some obvious conceptual problems, such as what assets may be comprised in the asserted percentage and how are the interests of third parties taken into account. However, on the view I take and the case ultimately put by the wife, these problems do not require exploration, beyond what is said in the following discussion.

“De facto” ownership

Before turning to consider the wife’s contentions in more detail, it is helpful to discuss the concept of property “of” a party to a marriage within s 79, often analysed in terms of what is usually called “de facto” ownership. In the context of s 79, there is a well-worn pathway in the jurisprudence which is often resorted to by litigants seeking to penetrate trust or corporate structures to claim assets held by such entities are “in reality” assets of a party to the marriage. In this, the statutory definition of property in s 4(1) of the Act has had an important role to play in allowing, in limited circumstances, what might be understood as a concept of property which can diverge from property recognised under the general law.
A representative example of this is found in the position of beneficiaries of a discretionary trust, like Z Trust. An individual object of a discretionary trust cannot claim any part of the trust fund or its income because they are not entitled to any interest in it, unless and until the trustees exercise their discretion in their favour; in that sense discretionary objects are in competition with each other for due consideration and “what the trustees give to one is his alone” (Dovgan & Dovgan [2021] FamCA 306 (“Dovgan”) at [299] and the authorities discussed there).
However, there is no doubt, on the basis of considerable authority in this and other courts, that assets owned in a trust or company structure may be found as a matter of fact, in circumstances of control by a spouse making the entity the spouse’s alter ego or “puppet”, to be the assets “of” that spouse and to fall within the specific statutory collocation “property of the parties to the marriage” in s 79 of the Act (Ascot Investments Pty Ltd v Harper (1981) 148 CLR 337 at 354–355; Spry at [64], [78]–[79], [91]; Dovgan at [270]–[274] and the authorities discussed there). It may be said that this line of authority explains the legal or equitable principles identifying some specific types of property relevant to a matrimonial cause. In accordance with it, the husband accepted the property held by the entities in category 1 should be taken to be his property.
A leading authority in the High Court is Spry. In that decision, French CJ pointed out that absent a specific application of discretionary trust capital or income to one of the objects of the trust, there was no equitable interest in its assets held by anyone, and “[t]here did not need to be” (at [49]). The question was whether interests in or in relation to the assets of the trust could answer the description of “property of the parties to the marriage” in s 79(1). He explained the word “property” is used in different ways in different statutory contexts and in s 79 is to be read as part of the collocation “property of the parties to the marriage”, widely and conformably with the purposes of the Act (Spry at [49], [51]–[52], [64], [79]).
The plurality in Spry observed that a discretionary trust power can fall within the concept of “property” for the purposes of s 79, albeit subject to the fiduciary duty to consider all beneficiaries, even though it may not be property according to the general law. I incorporate here my summary of this part of Spry given in Dovgan at [268]:
The plurality made clear that the power of a trustee to apply income or capital under the terms of the trust deed was not a species of property under the general law, but it can fall within the definition of “property” in s 4(1) of the Act as well as within the collocation "the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them" in s 79; on the other hand, the right of a beneficiary to due consideration and administration of the trust, which, as an equitable chose in action, is a species of personal property according to ordinary general law principles (at [48], [75], [79], [126]).
The decision in Spry was delivered in December 2008, shortly after the decision of White J in Public Trustee v Smith (2008) 1 ASTLR 488 (“Smith”) in May 2008. In Smith, White J at [110]–[124] had occasion to examine a number of authorities in this Court, such as Davidson and Davidson [1990] FamCA 187; (1991) FLC 92-197, where it had been held a party to a marriage had “de facto ownership” of assets otherwise held by a trustee of a trust. At [125] White J adverted specifically to the collocation “property of the parties to the marriage” in s 79 and explained, consistently with Spry:
It is perfectly understandable that in the context of s 79 the expression “property of the parties to the marriage or either of them” should be read as extending not only to property owned by a party to the marriage but also property controlled by a party to the marriage where the control is such as to put the party in the same position as if he or she were the owner of the property. That is how I understand the family law cases to have proceeded. In Marriage of Ashton and [Davidson and Davidson [1990] FamCA 187; (1991) FLC 92-197], the Court spoke of “de facto ownership”. Ownership is a legal concept. The expression “de facto ownership” appears to describe something which is not legal or equitable ownership but a power which is to be treated as the equivalent of ownership. It involves no stretching of the concept of property to construe the expression “property of a party” as extending to property which a party owns or which the party controls as if he or she were the owner. It comes down to what the word “of” in the phrase denotes – whether it means ownership only, or whether it includes control as effective as ownership. This is the context in which the family law cases must be read. In my view, they do not support the wider proposition that as a matter of general law an object of a discretionary trust can be described as the beneficial owner of the property held by the trustee, merely by virtue of his or her being a discretionary object and also controlling the trustee.
It is worth observing here that in Hall v Hall [2016] HCA 23; (2016) 257 CLR 490 at [54]–[55] the High Court also leaned upon the little word “of” when discussing the features which identify a party’s financial resource for the purpose of the Act:
... The requirement that the financial resource be that “of” a party no doubt implies that the source of financial support be one on which the party is capable of drawing. It must involve something more than an expectation of benevolence on the part of another. But it goes too far to suggest that the party must control the source of financial support. Thus, it has long correctly been recognised that a nominated beneficiary of a discretionary trust, who has no control over the trustee but who has a reasonable expectation that the trustee's discretion will be exercised in his or her favour, has a financial resource to the extent of that expectation.
Whether a potential source of financial support amounts to a financial resource of a party turns in most cases on a factual inquiry as to whether or not support from that source could reasonably be expected to be forthcoming were the party to call on it.
The Full Court cited the passage from Smith above in Harris & Dewell [2018] FamCAFC 94; (2018) 58 Fam LR 313 (“Harris & Dewell”) at [53] and went on to explain at [67]–[68] after a review of numerous earlier authorities, including Spry:
It should be accepted that the principles emerging from the High Court and from the decisions of this Court to which reference has been made permit of a finding that property ostensibly that of a trust can be treated as property of a party for s 79 purposes where evidence establishes that the person or entity in whom the trust deed vests effective control is the “puppet” or “creature” of that party. The metaphor is used to connote a situation where the person or entity with control (the “puppet”) does nothing without the party (the “puppet master”) controlling or directing that person or entity.
Control is not sufficient of itself. What is required is control over a person or entity who, by reason of the powers contained in the trust deed can obtain, or effect the obtaining of, a beneficial interest in the property of the trust. In our respectful view, it is in that sense, that Finn J speaks of “some lawful right to benefit from the assets of the trust” [in Stephens & Stephens & Ors (2007) FLC 93-336; [2007] FamCA 680 at 81,767–81,768].
Recently in Caldwell Carew J, after an extensive review of the authorities, made the following comments at [217], with which I respectfully agree, concerning the factors which may lead to the conclusion that trust assets were property of a spouse party under a discretionary trust:
Whether trust assets are property of a husband and/or wife within the meaning of s 79 depends on the circumstances of case. In particular, it may depend on the terms of any trust deed, the purpose of the trust, the origin of the trust assets, whether a spouse-party has control of the trust, whether that party has the power to distribute capital to one or other of the spouse-parties, the history of any dealings of trust property, and whether but for the trust, the property would unquestionably be the property of the spouse-party or spouse-parties. However, without the control of the trustee, whether direct or indirect, and a power to benefit by distributions one or other of the spouse-parties, there is little prospect, if any, of successfully arguing the trust assets are property for the purposes of s 79 [citing Harris & Dewell; Spry at [66], [126]–[134]; and Harris & Harris [1991] FamCA 124; (1991) FLC 92-254 at 78,706–78,708].
(Footnotes omitted)

A suite of rights

In line with notions of “de facto” ownership, the wife argued that when the “one transaction” approach is adopted, taken with representations of the husband’s ownership of property made by other individuals in the Z Corporate Group in loan applications to financiers on behalf of the husband (below at [242]), it justifies the conclusion that the husband enjoys a present property interest in 15 per cent of the assets held in the Z Corporate Group, commensurate with his 15 per cent shareholding in Z Pty Ltd, the trustee. To repeat, Mr Toscan valued this alleged property interest at $11,047,000. The wife contends for a valuation of $11,234,000, the difference relating to the treatment of taxation.
Then in her written submissions filed 28 August 2025, the wife developed this basic contention by submitting that the husband has a “suite of personal rights amounting to property which together comprise his interest in [Z Group]” (at paragraph 6). Her submissions were opaque about the precise identity or legal or equitable nature of the asserted property rights, apart from nominating the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd and his rights “as a primary beneficial object of [Z Trust]”. She argued that: “The Court should not artificially narrow or constrain the question of what property is held by the Husband or the value of that property by examining those rights in isolation” (wife’s written submissions filed 28 August 2025, paragraph 7).
I will consider “the suite of rights as property” arguments below. But it should be emphasised here that the wife’s submissions did not, as I understood it, explain unambiguously whether her claim about the “suite of rights” provided a basis to conclude the husband controlled and therefore owned 15 per cent of the Z Corporate Group, as opposed to the purported rights themselves being separate items of property with a value equivalent to 15 per cent of the Z Corporate Group. Even if such an argument was intended, it must also fail for the reasons discussed below at [252]–[310].
The husband devoted a considerable part of both his Case Outline and his written final submissions to meeting a case that the husband was a “de facto” owner of 15 per cent of the Z Corporate Group. By the end of the trial some of these submissions proved to be unnecessary.
This is because in oral submissions, senior counsel for the wife accepted the husband’s 15 per cent shareholding in the head entity, Z Pty Ltd, does not give him control “any more than it gives control to other members of his family”, thereby conceding authorities such as Spry and Harris & Dewell have no application, and eschewing an argument of “de facto” ownership through control of Z Pty Ltd or Z Trust. He conceded that, even on the wife’s argument, the Z Corporate Group acted through the Family Council, whose members were also shareholders and beneficiaries, and was not controlled by the husband.
But he argued this did not detract from the husband’s “suite of rights” as property. In answering his own question “what are the rights”, he submitted they are “part and parcel of the whole constitution of the Group”. He continued with a submission to the effect, as I best understood it, that because at the restructuring in 2007 Z Pty Ltd took on the role as trustee of an umbrella trust; it would “miss” the true construction of the husband’s property interest to divorce his rights as the shareholder of Z Pty Ltd and as a beneficiary of Z Trust “from the whole purpose and concept” at the time the Z Corporate Group was created.
With respect, these submissions were unequal to the task of clarifying what existing legal or equitable right or interest in property any of the “suite of rights” was claimed to be, or alternatively whether the suite of rights was said to be, in some way, a composite item of property. Taking refuge in broad allusions to a “suite of rights” embedded in the “whole constitution of the Group” and the “whole purpose and concept” of the restructure in 2007 did not help identify existing legal and equitable rights or interests in any property of the husband, according to ordinary legal and equitable principles or otherwise. Even allowing that the statutory definition of property in the Act is broad, to be read widely and conformably with the purposes of the Act, and for the purposes of s 79 particularly, has been held (above at [194]–[201], [219]–[227]) to have an extended reach beyond the general law in some limited circumstances, the wife’s arguments did not explain how the claimed “suite of rights” were property or fell into any such recognised extension, nor how the alleged property was a proprietary entitlement of the husband in “possession or reversion”.
The broad contention that the husband held a “suite of personal rights amounting to property” merely asserted a conclusion without explaining how it was reached. As will be described in more detail, this evasion underpinned all of the wife’s arguments about the interest of the husband in the Z Corporate Group, by assuming the alleged “suite of rights” were property and attempting to justify the assumption by purported and flawed methods of assigning a value to them.
Some further comment is warranted. The wife was correct to concede that construing the Franc Family constitution, the Family Council Constitution and Z Trust deed together as one transaction could not lead to the conclusion that the husband has “de facto ownership” of 15 per cent of Z Trust assets, according to existing authority.
The proper construction of a contract or trust instrument is to be determined objectively, requiring consideration of the text, the surrounding circumstances known to the parties and the purpose of the transaction or instrument (Pacific Carriers Ltd v BNP Paribas [2004] HCA 35; (2004) 218 CLR 451 at [22]; Toll (FGCT) Pty Limited v Alphapharm Pty Limited (2004) 219 CLR 165 at [40]).
The content of the constitutive documents was described in detail earlier. It is plain in my view that, upon an objective construction, the husband cannot be said to enjoy anything like the necessary level of control coupled with legal powers over Z Trust or Z Corporate Group assets generally by reason of powers in the relevant Z Trust deed, nor by reason of his 15 per cent shareholding in and directorship of the trustee company, Z Pty Ltd, nor as a result of any provision in the Z Pty Ltd or Family Council constitutions, including r 129, even taken as a “cognate whole”. It is clear that any decision by Z Pty Ltd as trustee to distribute income from Z Trust or to appoint its capital or trust assets, or as “head entity” of the Z Corporate Group, must be undertaken through the deliberations of the individuals comprising the board of Z Pty Ltd, influenced no doubt by other individuals from time to time on the Family Council, neither of which is controlled by the husband.
But none of this evidence alters the fact that there is no basis to conclude that assets held by Z Trust or subsidiary trusts and companies within the Z Corporate Group would be, in the absence of the trust structure, property of either spouse party and there is no basis in the evidence to conclude the wealth held in the Z Corporate Group, or any particular portion of it, exists as a direct or primary result of efforts or contributions of either spouse party. This is entirely different to the position in Spry (see [78]–[80], [126], [137]). The Z Trust, Z Group and the Z Corporate Group have been active for decades in property development. It is undisputed that the wealth held in the Z Corporate Group is comprised of numerous assets outside the marriage and must be the result of profuse investment and business activities and decisions over many years by not just the husband, but by a number of other individuals, with or without the husband, who were and are strangers to the marriage, none of whom are parties to the proceedings and whose interests could be affected (Barrett & Winnie [2022] FedCFamC1A 99; (2022) FLC 94-093 (“Barrett & Winnie”) at 81,477). To paraphrase French CJ in Spry, the situation in this case is one involving a combination of “purposes and family and extraneous assets” (at [69]). I also repeat the point made by the Full Court at [15] in B Pty Ltd & Anker (above at [191]), “the rights and duties which the ... Court may validly create or define under [s 79] are confined to those which have their basis in the marital relationship”.
The constitutive documents also do not, through a suite of rights, “bind” Z Pty Ltd to make distributions of income or capital to the shareholders of Z Pty Ltd in accordance with their percentage shareholding. There can be little doubt that the exercise of a discretionary trust power to appoint trust assets is fiduciary (Baba v Sheehan [2021] NSWCA 58 per Brereton JA at [3]). A fiduciary discretion must be exercised in good faith, upon real and genuine consideration and in accordance with the purposes for which the discretion was conferred (Karger v Paul [1984] VicRp 13; [1984] VR 161; Australian Prudential Regulation Authority v Kelaher [2019] FCA 1521; (2019) 138 ACSR 459). A “real or genuine consideration” by a trustee requires that there be “the exercise of an active discretion” and that there be consideration of whether or not to exercise the power (Owies v JJE Nominees Pty Ltd (in its capacity as the trustee for the Owies Family Trust) [2022] VSCA 142 at [86]). The trust discretions must be exercised personally without taking into account improper, irrelevant or irrational considerations, and not in conjunction with, or under the direction of, somebody else (Scaffidi v Montevento Holdings Pty Ltd (2011) 6 ASTLR 446 at [150]). The suggestion that “the suite of rights” bind Z Pty Ltd in the manner suggested must assume that there will be no genuine consideration by Z Pty Ltd as the trustee or that the constitutive documents construed together have placed an illegal fetter upon the exercise of its discretionary fiduciary power by requiring in advance its exercise in a specific manner in favour of a specific beneficiary, namely, the husband. The clear requirement for a deliberative process in decision making by a group of individuals controlling the trustee, as a matter of construction of the “cognate whole”, discussed above at [237]–[238], leaves no room for a conclusion that Z Pty Ltd was bound as alleged.
I also return to the amended version of s 79. As mentioned, s 79(3)(a)(i) now imposes a statutory obligation upon the Court to identify the “existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property” of the parties to the marriage or either of them. There may be some doubt whether earlier authorities which relied upon a concept of “de facto ownership” survive the new version of s 79. The amended wording of s 79(3) raises the possibility that a purposive construction of the statutory definition of property in s 4(1) together with s 79(3) favours a more constrained recognition, or even rejection, of “de facto” ownership. As mentioned, the definition in s 4(1) refers to an in entitlement “in possession or reversion”. A discretionary trustee may have an entitlement “in possession” to trust assets, but a discretionary object does not, on any view (Spry at [48], [156]–[158]), and usually neither has an entitlement “in reversion”, meaning a reversionary interest or remainder. As White J observed in Smith, “de facto ownership”, if it means anything, describes a level of control or power “to be treated as the equivalent of ownership” but is not, by definition, legal or equitable ownership. A judicially determined equivalence to ownership can only with considerable conceptual difficulty fall within the statutory embrace of an existing proprietary entitlement “in possession or reversion”. If so, this is a further reason why any reliance by the wife on “de facto” ownership must fail. Since neither party’s submissions took any point about the impact of the amendments to s 79, I refrain from expressing a concluded view.
It is necessary here to deal with a body of evidence upon which the wife relied, beyond a construction of the constitutive documents as one transaction, as factual material supporting her contentions about the husband’s de facto ownership of Z Corporate Group assets. She relied upon representations of control or ownership in some commercial documents (Exhibits K, O, Q, T, EE and FF). In summary, these were created in 2024 in support of the husband’s applications for financial accommodation. They contain representations by individuals other than the husband about his financial position and sources of income. For example, Exhibits O and T, as explained by Mr AW, simply proffer a forecast of potential distributions to the husband. Mr AW gave unchallenged evidence that the husband had received no distributions from Z Trust since 2018. Exhibit K, construed most favourably to the wife on its face, contains an assertion that the husband has ownership of one third of the assets of the BQ Trust. The assertion comes from a Ms DM, who prepared Exhibit K, not the husband or any director or shareholder in Z Pty Ltd.
The husband objected to Exhibits K, O, EE and FF on the ground of relevance or in the alternative, sought orders excluding them pursuant to s 135 of the Evidence Act. I accept the husband’s submission that these documents cannot be used as extrinsic evidence to construe the proper meaning of the constitutive documents or any of them individually and are not relevant for that purpose. In light of the way the wife ultimately put her case about the husband’s interest in the Z Corporate Group, these documents are irrelevant to those issues. They do not have any persuasive value in supporting the claim of the wife that the husband’s “suite of rights” are property. In light of Mr Toscan’s expert evidence they also add nothing of importance to questions of value.
However, they are relevant to the Z Corporate Group and Z Trust as a financial resource of the husband, and are admissible for that reason. Although this was not in issue, if the wife’s arguments about property failed, in my view the documents the subject of objection have some relevance to the nature and detail of the husband’s financial resources. For the same reasons, I decline to uphold the husband’s application to exclude the documents pursuant to s 135.
After considering the wife’s submissions carefully, and after attempting to penetrate the amorphous “suite of rights” contentions, the only category of legal or equitable rights and interests in property known to ordinary legal and equitable principles, into which such “suite of rights” could conceivably fall, is choses in action.
Choses in possession are distinguished from choses in action by the characteristic that the chose can be held in actual physical possession and title may be transferred by delivery, in the technical sense of that term (Gamer’s Motor Centre (Newcastle) Proprietary Limited v Natwest Wholesale Australia Proprietary Limited [1987] HCA 30; (1987) 163 CLR 236 at 244–247, 255, 260–263, 273–274). Standard authorities such as Torkington v Magee [1902] 2 KB 427 at 430 describe choses in action as follows: “[All] personal rights of property which can only be claimed or enforced by action, and not by taking physical possession”. But choses in action are personal property generally having some juristic equivalence to chattels (White v Shortall [2006] NSWSC 1379; (2006) 68 NSWLR 650 (“White v Shortall”) at [174]). Although they are incorporeal, this characteristic may qualify them as a proprietary entitlement “in possession” within s 4(1). They can be legal or equitable, and include shares, debts, judgment debts, negotiable instruments, rights as a discretionary object, an equitable mortgage, a partner’s interest in partnership assets and rights enforceable by action (or causes of action) (Krishell Pty Ltd v Nilant [2006] WASCA 223; (2006) 32 WAR 540 at [73]; RP Meagher, JD Heydon and MJ Leeming, Meagher, Gummow & Lehane’s Equity: Doctrines and Remedies (Butterworths LexisNexis, 4th edition, 2002) [6–005]).
In National Provincial Bank Ltd v Ainsworth [1965] UKHL 1; [1965] AC 1175 at 1247–1248, Lord Wilberforce explained:
Before a right or an interest can be admitted into the category of property, or of a right affecting property, it must be definable, identifiable by third parties, capable in its nature of assumption by third parties, and have some degree of permanence or stability.
A right connected to enforcement by court action is central to the concept of a chose in action, something that is capable of being turned into money (Loxton v Moir [1914] HCA 89; (1914) 18 CLR 360 at 379 per Rich J). The existence of a remedy is an essential condition for the existence of the chose in action, although the remedy is not property itself (Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society [1997] UKHL 28; [1998] 1 WLR 896 at 915 per Lord Hoffman).
The capacity to be assigned is often recognised as an attribute of a proprietary right, which is consistent with a capacity “in its nature of assumption by third parties” and being held in possession. The High Court has said choses in action in general are assignable other than “bare right to litigate” such as a cause of action in tort (Poulton v The Commonwealth [1953] HCA 101; (1952) 89 CLR 540 at 571, 602). This attribute places them within the long-established common law model of proprietary interests (Greg Tolhurst, The Assignment of Contractual Rights (Bloomsbury, 2nd edition, 2016) [2.05]). Even if alienability is not a necessary element of a proprietary interest (Mullane above at [196]), it has been said by the Full Court that if a right to sue is both unassignable and personal, it lacks a proprietary character, referring to claims for personal damages in tort (Zorbas and Zorbas (1990) FLC 92-160; Best and Best [1993] FamCA 107; (1993) FLC 92-418 at 80,287–80,288). However, as explained below at [269], the better view now appears to be that rights to sue, even tortious claims, are property, at least for the purposes of many statutory definitions of property.
Ultimately, arising from the contentions of the wife, there appeared to be possibly three recognisable choses in action as proprietary interests, attributable to the husband in relation to the Z Corporate Group. Firstly, the husband enjoys an equitable chose in action as a discretionary object of Z Trust, and subsidiary trusts, although for the purposes of describing applicable principles, each trust does not require separate consideration, and Z Trust can be treated as the representative example. Secondly, r 129 itself may embody a chose in action as a contractual right. Thirdly, the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd constitutes a bundle of choses in action. As I understood the wife’s argument, she claimed each of these choses should be valued at 15 per cent of Z Corporate Group either because they have attached to them the right to withdraw in r 129 or perhaps a right to 15 per cent of the assets held on trust, or as a “suite of rights” they formed a species of property to be valued at 15 per cent of Z Corporate Group.
The husband argues there is no such property, rather his interest in the Z Corporate Group is a financial resource, to be taken into account under s 79(5)(c). This was undisputed, if the wife’s contentions about property were unsuccessful.
For the following reasons I am not persuaded the “suite of rights” constitute property and I reject the wife’s contentions in this regard.

Choses in Action

Equitable chose in action

The first possibility to consider is the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficiary for due consideration and administration of Z Trust, characterised in Spry as an equitable chose in action. Although in final submissions the wife appeared to abandon any reliance on this as an item of property, or as “the interest of the husband” in the Z Corporate Group, as explained earlier (above at [92]), Mr Toscan said he was valuing the husband’s interest as a discretionary object. This bespeaks a level of confusion which necessitates some consideration of the equitable chose in action, if only to provide some context to the wife’s other submissions about r 129.
A discretionary object’s equitable chose in action has indicia of property but some authorities have concluded that it could not be considered an asset because it lacked the requisite attribute of value: e.g. R & I Bank of Western Australia Ltd v Anchorage Investments Pty Ltd (1993) 10 WAR 59 at 79–80. This is because a discretionary beneficiary has no interest in trust property until an exercise of discretion in their favour (Swishette Pty Ltd and Another v Australian Competition and Consumer Commission [2017] FCAFC 45; (2017) 249 FCR 483 at [26]). The husband argued he only enjoyed an expectancy as a discretionary beneficiary, which was not property and had no value.
But in Spry, French CJ at [78] held that a discretionary object’s equitable chose in action is property, although he remarked upon the difficulty of valuing such an asset. However, he seemed to accept it was possible and difficulty in valuing such a chose in action does not of itself deny its quality as property. In the circumstances of Spry, the wife’s equitable chose in action together with the husband’s fiduciary power as trustee to appoint the entire trust assets to the wife compelled the conclusion that the value of the equitable chose in action was the same as the value of the trust assets (at [137]). But those circumstances were unusual, and provide limited guidance on the question of value in the context of other discretionary trusts, particularly in the context of a complex group of commercial trading trusts.
Some other authorities suggest guidance of more general application. In Simmons & Simmons [2008] FamCA 1088; (2008) 40 Fam LR 520 at [122] after referring to Spry, Watt J accepted the possibility of valuing such an equitable chose in action saying, “the existence of a longstanding scheme of distributions to beneficiaries such as the husband and his siblings provides a useful starting point in the valuation process”. In Dovgan at [299]–[301] this led me to conclude the weight of authority supports a discretionary object owning an equitable chose in action as having the status of personal property, albeit hard to value, but that the underlying trust assets could bear at best no more than indirectly upon the value, if any, of such a chose in action. Rather any value was equivalent to valuation of a chance and “looks to the possibility that after due consideration, the trustee’s discretion will be exercised in an object’s favour” (Dovgan at [300]). In Barrett & Winnie at [155] the Full Court adverted to the possibility of valuing a discretionary object’s chose in action, but no evidence of value was provided to the trial judge.
What these authorities do make clear is that the husband’s equitable chose in action as a discretionary object is a separate item of property but it gives him no interest in Z Trust assets and could not carry a value derived directly from their value, especially where it is conceded he was not the trustee, does not control the trustee and could not appoint the entire trust assets either to himself or the wife, unlike in Spry. So if his equitable chose in action can be valued at all, which cannot be regarded as settled, the valuer could perhaps use actuarial methods, as suggested by French CJ in Spry, or have regard to patterns of distribution or the chance of the discretion being exercised in favour of the relevant discretionary object. No expert evidence was adduced, attempting to attribute a value using such a methodology, possibly because it cannot be done. There is nothing in the constitutive documents, or the overarching purpose and intention of the Z Corporate Group structure, whether or not divined from the “one transaction” approach to construction of the constitutive documents, which changes this reality.
I also record that the pattern of distributions disclosed in the evidence bore no obvious relationship to the respective shareholdings of Franc family members in Z Pty Ltd, nor that patterns of distribution particularly favoured the husband. As the husband argued, the evidence shows distributions to the husband since 2007 total about $2,100,000 while his stepfather received $32,200,000, and neither figure bears any obvious relationship to their respective shareholdings (15 per cent held by the husband and 40 per cent held jointly by his stepfather and mother). Such distributions are consistent with trustee deliberations leading to an exercise of discretion unrelated to the proportion of shareholding.
This brings the discussion to the expert evidence of Mr Toscan. As mentioned earlier, he was asked to value the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficial object of Z Trust. He explained in his report that he valued this interest “as defined in and governed by the [Franc] Family Constitution and the Constitution of the [Franc] Family Council as amended in 2022”, which meant considering the husband’s right to “realise a lump-sum value for his interest via the ‘Exit’ mechanisms outlined at rule 129 of the [Franc] Family Constitution” (Toscan report, paragraphs 24 to 26). So, despite purporting to value the husband’s interest as a discretionary object, Mr Toscan actually simply applied the formula in r 129 to derive a value for the Z Corporate Group and then attributed 15 per cent of that value to the husband’s “interest”. Accordingly, Mr Toscan gave no value to the equitable chose in action derived from a pattern of distribution or an assessment of the husband’s chance of an exercise of the trustee’s discretion in his favour.
I am not bound to accept Mr Toscan’s value even if he is the only expert (Federal Commissioner of Taxation v St Helens Farm (A.C.T.) Pty Ltd [1981] HCA 4; (1981) 146 CLR 336 at 381). This applies equally in this Court where a single expert has been appointed pursuant to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth): Salmon & Salmon [2020] FamCAFC 134 at [42]. Valuation should be a common sense endeavour after consideration of all material to fix a value satisfactory to the mind of the Court (Phillips and Phillips [2002] FamCA 350; (2002) FLC 93-104 at [44] citing The Commonwealth v Milledge [1953] HCA 6; (1953) 90 CLR 157 at 161–162).
The problem here is that the relevant asserted “interest” of the husband in the Z Corporate Group was not precisely identified. It seems clear that Mr Toscan conflated the husband’s equitable chose in action as a discretionary object with his contractual right or rights under r 129, on the basis of the way he was instructed. In my view, this was flawed. As mentioned earlier (above at [127]), there is a fundamental and mutually exclusive difference between the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd and his equitable chose in action as a discretionary object of Z Trust (and subsidiary trusts) on the one hand, and the right to elect to withdraw with a payment bestowed by r 129. The former items of property only continue as property of the husband if he remains as a participant in the Z Corporate Group, while the right to payment arising from r 129 is contingent on him relinquishing his shares in Z Pty Ltd and leaving the Z Corporate Group. I discuss r 129 further below, and conclude that it does not constitute presently existing property, but even if it does, it cannot be securely valued. Here the point is that conflating r 129 with the husband’s other choses in action for the purposes of valuation presumes, but does not explain, some proper connection between them and is apt to mislead for this reason.
As mentioned, in her oral submissions the wife conceded there was no evidence of the value of the equitable chose in action because the husband’s “interest in the group” had been valued a different way, meaning by reference to r 129. It is here that the wife’s submissions also became somewhat obscure. In her written submissions she endorsed Mr Toscan’s methodology because it gave a value to the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd. Again, I consider the shares below. But then the wife continued:
If anything, Mr Toscan’s opinion is bolstered when one correctly takes the broader view that what is to be valued is not merely those shares, but the entire suite of his rights in [Z Trust] including his corollary interest as a primary beneficial object of [Z Pty Ltd].

(Wife’s written submissions filed 28 August 2025, paragraph 16)
This submission appeared either to presume that the husband’s equitable chose in action gave him a present proprietary interest in 15 per cent of the assets of the Z Corporate Group, calling it “his corollary interest as a primary beneficial object”, or meant it was one of the husband’s “suite” of interests, along with the shares having, collectively, a value of 15 per cent of the Z Corporate Group’s assets. This is another permutation of the “de facto” ownership argument which the wife otherwise disavowed. Having conceded the husband does not have any of the control or powers over assets through Z Pty Ltd which would be necessary, according to authorities discussed above at [220]–[227], the wife’s argument cannot get any stronger or produce a different outcome by linking the husband’s equitable chose in action to the shares in Z Pty Ltd, or to an indeterminate “suite of rights” or r 129 specifically.
To the extent the evidence of Mr Toscan should be understood as attributing a value to the husband’s equitable chose in action, I do not accept it. I conclude there is no evidence upon which to give a value to it. It will be taken into account under s 79(5) as a financial resource.

Rule 129 – inchoate chose in action

The second possibility is that r 129 creates a right which is itself property. Although the wife in her oral submissions seemed to focus on the husband’s shares in Z Pty Ltd as an item of property, rather than r 129 as a chose in action, some discussion of r 129 and its possible status as property seems inevitable because of the wife’s reliance on an imprecise “suite of rights”, its use by Mr Toscan to produce a valuation and the resort to r 129 as a basis to give a value to the husband’s shares.
Any relationship between the husband’s shares and his right pursuant to r 129 does not dissolve the potential status of each as separate items of property. In White v Shortall Campbell J explained at [197]–[199]:
Some of the rights to sue the company that a shareholder has exist simply by virtue of having the status of shareholder, regardless of the number of shares held. Such rights include rights to receive the information that statute requires shareholders to be given, the right to be given notice of and to attend at certain meetings of the company, and the right to vote at certain company meetings. Other rights that a shareholder has to sue the company are ones that a shareholder has proportionately to the number of shares held -- such as the right to a dividend, to a return of capital, or to vote on a poll at the meeting. Some of the rights of a shareholder to sue the company arise by virtue of the contract contained in the company’s constitution. Other rights of a shareholder to sue the company – including some very important ones – might arise directly by statute (e.g. rights to receive accounts and reports, to join in a requisition of a company general meeting, or to appoint a proxy). Other rights that any shareholder has in a company by virtue of the status of being a shareholder can arise from a contract arising separately to the company’s constitution (e.g. if the company in question holds itself out as willing to provide goods or services to a shareholder at a special discounted price).
Some of the rights that attach to shares are themselves inherently assignable, even in circumstances where the shares themselves are not assigned – it is possible to make an equitable assignment of the right to receive dividends from a particular parcel of shares in particular years (by analogy with Shepherd v The Commissioner of Taxation of the Commonwealth of Australia [1965] HCA 70; (1965) 113 CLR 385), and possible to make an equitable assignment of the right to receive some particular measure of money on a particular type of return of capital. Such assignments can take place only because the rights assigned are themselves treated as property. And the only type of property they could be is choses in action. Thus, some of the rights that a shareholder has by virtue of being a shareholder are themselves items of property that are separate to the item of property that constitutes the share itself.
And it is not only fractional rights in a share that are capable of being seen as separate items of property. For rights of the type where the number of shares provides the measure by reference to which a shareholder’s right against the company is calculated, the chose in action can be seen as being the right to sue the company to receive some particular type of benefit. For example, when the holder of 1000 shares in a company sues to recover a dividend that has been declared but is unpaid, there is just one action that the shareholder brings, to recover the dividend – there are not 1000 separate rights to be paid a dividend: cf Marlborough Harbour Board v Charter Travel Co Ltd (1989) 18 NSWLR 223 at 231. In that way, the chose in action – the thing that the law regards as a piece of property because it can be sued for – is the single right to be paid the dividend, the measure of which is the number of shares held.
The elements of r 129 are summarised above at [125]. To recap, r 129 gives a Primary Family Member, including the husband, and a Related Family Sub-Group, a right to payment arising upon the exercise of a right to issue a Withdrawal Notice and subject to certain exercises of discretion by the directors concerning the timing of payment. The wife asserted it was impossible to read the constitutive documents as a cognate whole to mean that the husband's right to payment on withdrawal was limited to 15 per cent of Z Pty Ltd assets, but they must be construed as meaning 15 per cent of the entirety of the assets of Z Corporate Group. I accept this much is correct, and also it is clear from the wording of r 129.1.
The husband’s right to issue a Withdrawal Notice enlivening a right to payment under r 129, if existing property, could only be property of an incorporeal nature, requiring characterisation as a contractual chose in action derived from the Franc Family constitution, if it met the description as a personal right of property which can “only be claimed or enforced by action, and not by taking physical possession”.
Accepted principle treats a contract as a composite bundle of separate choses or a composite chose in action (Barre & Barre [2021] FamCA 101 at [200]–[201]). A right to contractual performance is recognised in some areas of legal discourse, such as assignments or the tort of interference with contractual relations, as a chose in action, or a proprietary right, with similarities to a chose in possession (Zhu v Treasurer of NSW [2004] HCA 56; (2004) 218 CLR 530 at 575–576, [130]).
